reduction: Replace the local copies with `use j2k_test_support::fnv1a64_hex;` (and lift fnv1a64_hex_slices into this crate if the multi-slice variant is worth sharing).
verify_note: Verified the canonical `pub fn fnv1a64_hex` at lib.rs:283 and 5 duplicate re-implementations at j2k-cuda/benches/htj2k_decode.rs:962, htj2k_encode.rs:982, j2k-compare/src/bin/jp2k_fixture_compare.rs:3807, jp2k_encode_compare.rs:2769, and j2k-compare/tests/bench_harness.rs:859. The 3 simple copies (cuda decode/encode benches, bench_harness) are byte-identical to the canonical body. The 2 compare-bin copies define fnv1a64_hex as a thin wrapper over a local fnv1a64_hex_slices; the single-slice path is functionally identical output, so replacement is behavior-preserving. Dependency wiring already exists: j2k-compare has j2k-test-support under [dependencies] (Cargo.toml:15, already imported at jp2k_fixture_compare.rs:28), and j2k-cuda has it under [dev-dependencies] (Cargo.toml:55/57) which is available to benches. So all copies are safely replaceable with `use j2k_test_support::fnv1a64_hex;`, and lifting fnv1a64_hex_slices into the shared crate covers the multi-slice variant. Caveats: the title says '7x' but there are only 6 total implementations (5 duplicates + 1 canonical) — inflated. est_loc_saved of 45 is slightly high; realistic net saving is ~35-40 after accounting for added imports and lifting fnv1a64_hex_slices (~10 lines) into the shared crate.

title: Duplicated const + fn double-API for embedded JPEG fixtures
description: Each embedded JPEG/reference fixture is exposed twice: as a pub const (e.g. JPEG_BASELINE_444_8X8) and as a pub fn that simply returns CONST.to_vec() (jpeg_baseline_444_8x8). Consumers split inconsistently across the two forms (consts used 16/12/4x, several fns used only 0-1x), doubling the surface and re-export list for no behavioral gain.
evidence: fixtures.rs pairs const+fn for 10 fixtures; usage counts: JPEG_BASELINE_420_16X16 const=16 but fn jpeg_baseline_420_16x16 low; jpeg_baseline_444_8x8_rgb fn=1 while JPEG_BASELINE_444_8X8_RGB const=0; jpeg_baseline_420_16x16_rgb fn=0 (dead, see finding 1). All are thin `.to_vec()` wrappers.
reduction: Keep a single canonical form (the &[u8] consts) and migrate the few fn callers, removing the ~10 trivial wrapper fns and their re-export entries.

title: Copy-pasted 8-bit/16-bit x 4:2:2/4:2:0 lossless entropy encoder families
description: The lossless-JPEG fixture generator carries near-identical parallel implementations that differ only in sample width (u8 vs u16) and chroma layout (4:2:2 vs 4:2:0): lossless_422_entropy_8bit / _16bit / _with_restarts, lossless_420_entropy_8bit / _16bit / _with_restarts, encode_lossless_component_sample_8bit / _16bit (+ _with_restart), lossless_predicted_value / _16bit, upsample_h2v1/h2v2_8bit / _16bit, lossless_4xx_planes_to_rgb8 / _rgb16. Each pair repeats the same predictor/category/bit-packing logic.
evidence: rg of `^fn ` in jpeg_fixtures.rs shows the matched 8bit/16bit and 422/420 pairs across lines 2149-2500 (entropy), 2522-2683 (mcu/sample encoders), 2786-2919 (predicted_value), 3106-3211 (upsample), 2988-3105 (planes_to_rgb). Bodies differ only in the scalar type and shift constants.
reduction: Introduce a small sample trait (or generic over an unsigned integer + max-value) so the 8/16-bit variants collapse to one implementation, and parameterize 4:2:2/4:2:0 by subsampling factors, removing roughly half of these ~1000 lines.

title: jpeg_fixtures.rs is a 3930-line god-file
description: A single module mixes unrelated fixture families (12-bit extended baseline, progressive, SOF3 lossless with 3 subsamplings x 2 bit-depths, 4-component/CMYK, restart variants) together with their private entropy-coding internals (bit packing, predictor math, YCbCr conversion, chroma upsampling). This is far past the crate's own 'split files before they become hard to review' guidance and makes the duplication above hard to see and safely refactor.
evidence: wc -l = 3930; rg of pub fn / fn shows 130+ pub fixtures plus ~60 private helpers spanning entropy codecs, colorspace conversion, and upsampling all in one file.
reduction: Split into submodules (e.g. jpeg_fixtures/{extended12, progressive, lossless, four_component} + a shared jpeg_entropy internal module), which also makes the 8/16-bit generic consolidation tractable.

title: weights.rs re-implements j2k-transcode's wavelet lifting math verbatim
location: j2k-transcode-metal/src/weights.rs:5-294 (forward_lift_97, linearized_97_from_sample_slice, linearized_53_from_sample_slice, ALPHA/BETA/GAMMA/DELTA/KAPPA/INV_KAPPA, low_len/high_len, Dwt97OneDimensional/Dwt53OneDimensional)
description: The 9/7 forward lifting kernel, the 5/3 and 9/7 sample-slice linearizers, all six lifting coefficients, and the low_len/high_len helpers are copied byte-for-byte (only local variable names n/i vs sample_count/sample_idx differ) from j2k-transcode/src/dct97_2d.rs and dct53_2d.rs. The two crates also both define private structs named Dwt97OneDimensional/Dwt53OneDimensional/Dwt53WeightRows/SparseWeightRow computing the same weight rows two different ways.
evidence: weights.rs:210-276 forward_lift_97 is identical to j2k-transcode/src/dct97_2d.rs:326-372; weights.rs:174-198 linearized_53_from_sample_slice is identical to j2k-transcode/src/dct53_2d.rs:422-446; constants ALPHA=-1.586134342059924 etc. duplicated at weights.rs:5-10 and dct97_2d.rs:13; rg shows forward_lift_97/linearized_97_from_sample_slice defined in both dct97_2d.rs and weights.rs.
reduction: Expose the lifting primitives (forward_lift_97, linearized_53/97_from_sample_slice, coefficients, low_len/high_len) from j2k-transcode as pub(crate)->pub or a shared internal module and have weights.rs's Sparse*WeightRows::for_len build on them instead of re-deriving the math.
verify_note: Verified byte-for-byte equivalence. metal/weights.rs:210-276 forward_lift_97 is identical to transcode/dct97_2d.rs:326-372 (only loop var names differ: sample_idx vs i, semantics identical). weights.rs:174-198 linearized_53_from_sample_slice is line-identical to dct53_2d.rs:422-446. Constants ALPHA=-1.586134342059924 etc. match dct97_2d.rs:13. low_len/high_len and the Dwt97/53OneDimensional structs are duplicated too. The metal crate already depends on j2k-transcode (Cargo.toml:23 path=../j2k-transcode), so exposing these primitives (currently private fns; the structs/linearized_97 are #[cfg(test)] in transcode and would need un-gating) is feasible and safe. Removable from weights.rs: constants(6)+forward_lift_97(67)+linearized_97(9)+linearized_53(25)+low/high_len(7)+structs(9) ~=123, net ~110 after imports. Estimate is accurate.

title: metal.rs is a 2607-line god-file with 97 functions
description: A single module holds runtime setup, 5/3 and 9/7 single + batch + code-block dispatch pipelines, param structs, resident-handoff validation, geometry helpers, buffer alloc/readback, and IDCT basis tables. This concentration is what drives the copy-paste findings below and makes the crate hard to review.
evidence: wc -l = 2607; rg counts 97 fn definitions plus ~25 param/geometry structs; single `mod metal`.
reduction: Split into submodules (e.g. metal/runtime.rs, metal/dwt53.rs, metal/dwt97.rs, metal/resident.rs, metal/buffers.rs) so the shared helpers can be factored and the duplicated pipelines collapsed.
verify_note: The factual claims check out: wc -l=2607 and rg counts exactly 97 fn definitions and 27 structs in a single module. However this is an architecture/reorg finding, not a genuine code reduction. Splitting a file into submodules does not reduce LOC by itself; it adds module declarations and re-imports (net-positive lines). The cited 40 LOC saving is attributed to 'the duplicated pipelines collapsed', which is exactly finding #3 -- so the estimate double-counts other findings rather than representing savings from the split. The defect (oversized file) is real but the reduction as described yields ~0 standalone LOC.

title: Two staged-batch dispatch pipelines share nearly all structure
description: The dwt97 staged batch and the htj2k97 code-block batch functions perform the same sequence: pack/upload blocks, allocate row buffers, allocate band buffers, timestamp, validate resident dct/dwt handoffs, staged row lift, staged column lift, readback with identical timing bookkeeping. The htj2k variant only adds a quantize step and a different readback.
evidence: Lines 613-638 vs 673-710 are line-for-line parallel (pack_upload_start/us/transfers/bytes, resident_dct/dwt_handoff_count, row_start/idct_row_lift_us, column_start/column_lift_us, readback_start/readback_us/transfers/bytes).
reduction: Extract a shared helper that runs pack->row-lift->column-lift and fills the shared Dwt97BatchStageTimings fields, leaving each caller to add its band/quantize/readback specialization.
verify_note: Read metal.rs:605-713. The two functions are structurally parallel and share truly identical lines: pack_upload_us/transfers/bytes timing (618-620 vs 680-682), row-lift block with dispatch_dwt97_staged_row_lift + timing (626-628 vs 688-690), and column-lift block with dispatch_dwt97_staged_column_lift + timing (630-632 vs 692-694) -- these dispatch calls are the SAME functions. But the surrounding code genuinely differs: blocks buffers differ (dwt97_batch_blocks_buffer vs dwt97_codeblock_batch_blocks_buffer), handoff validation differs (validate_*_for_dwt97_jobs vs _for_htj2k_jobs, typed on distinct job types), the codeblock path allocates extra codeblock_buffers, and the tails diverge (band readback vs quantize+codeblock readback). A shared helper is feasible via generics/closures but must plumb the differing buffer/validation callbacks, which re-adds boilerplate. Only ~9-12 lines are truly identical duplicate; est_loc_saved of 45 is inflated ~2.5x. Realistic net ~15-18.

title: ProjectedBands -> Dwt53/Dwt97TwoDimensional 8-field copy repeated 4x
description: The same 8-field struct copy (ll/hl/lh/hh/low_width/low_height/high_width/high_height) from the local ProjectedBands into Dwt53TwoDimensional/Dwt97TwoDimensional is hand-written four times.
evidence: Four identical field-by-field literals at the listed ranges; ProjectedBands is a local struct (metal.rs:1123) so a From/helper is feasible.
reduction: Add `impl From<ProjectedBands> for Dwt97TwoDimensional<f64>` (and 5/3) or a small `fn into_two_dimensional(bands)` helper and call it at all four sites.

title: Accelerator constructors duplicate ~22 field initializers each
description: new_explicit() and for_auto() each spell out all ~22 struct fields; only `mode` and 6 threshold defaults differ between them. The 20 counter/state fields are initialized to 0/None/false in both.
evidence: Fields reversible_dwt53_attempts..min_auto_dwt97_batch_samples plus session are written identically in both bodies (lib.rs:363-379 vs 394-410).
reduction: Keep one private base constructor (or derive Default for the counters via a helper) and have new_explicit/for_auto override only mode + the six threshold fields.

title: Per-method non-macos placeholder match repeated in every trait method
description: Every accelerator method contains an identical `#[cfg(not(target_os = "macos"))]` block doing `match self.mode { Explicit => Err(DeviceUnavailable), Auto => Ok(None) }` (some with a `let _ = job;` binding).
evidence: Six near-identical cfg blocks at the listed ranges.
reduction: Add one `fn unavailable_dispatch<T>(&self) -> Result<Option<T>, TranscodeStageError>` (with a param-drop helper) and call it from each cfg(not(macos)) arm.
verify_note: Verified all six cfg(not(target_os=macos)) blocks at lib.rs:627-633, 680-686, 723-730, 759-766, 812-819, 869-876. Each is the same match self.mode { Explicit => Err(DeviceUnavailable), Auto => Ok(None) } (some with a preceding let _ = (params) drop). All six trait methods return Result<Option<T>>, so a single fn unavailable_dispatch<T>(&self)->Result<Option<T>,TranscodeStageError> plus a param-drop is type-compatible and behavior-preserving. Each block is 7-8 lines; reducing to ~2 inner lines saves ~4-5 per site x6 minus a ~5-line helper = net ~19. Estimate of 20 is accurate.

title: Metal accelerator wrapper near-duplicates the CUDA accelerator wrapper
description: MetalDctToWaveletStageAccelerator mirrors CudaDctToWaveletStageAccelerator: same counter field set, same 12 `pub const fn *_attempts/_dispatches` getters, same with_auto_*_thresholds builders, same Auto/Explicit dispatch-decline contract. Both are thin per-backend wrappers of the same trait.
evidence: rg: both crates expose exactly 12 attempts/dispatches getters; both define new_explicit/for_auto/with_auto_reversible_batch_thresholds/with_auto_dwt97_batch_thresholds with matching signatures.
reduction: Consider a shared generic dispatch-bookkeeping struct in j2k-transcode (counters + threshold gating + Auto-decline logic) parameterized over a backend dispatch trait, leaving each crate only the backend-specific dispatch calls.
verify_note: Confirmed both crates define the same 12 counter getters (reversible_dwt53_attempts/dispatches, ..._batch_..., dwt53_, dwt97_, dwt97_batch_, htj2k97_codeblock_batch_ attempts/dispatches) as identical pub const fn field accessors (metal lib.rs:516-582 vs cuda lib.rs:213-281) differing only in doc wording, plus identical with_auto_reversible_batch_thresholds and with_auto_dwt97_batch_thresholds builders. That is ~58 duplicated lines per crate (~116 total). Consolidating via a shared bookkeeping struct/macro in j2k-transcode is feasible and safe for the pure counter/threshold data. Caveat: the finding's evidence overstates the ctors -- metal has new_explicit_with_session/for_auto_with_session/_with_device, NOT the plain new_explicit/for_auto that CUDA has, so those are NOT mergeable; and cross-crate consolidation touches the published (=0.6.2) public API. The getter/threshold duplication is genuine and reducible by roughly the claimed amount.

title: Redundant inherent dct_grid_to_reversible_dwt53_batch plus trivial buffer_with_slice wrapper
description: The accelerator exposes a public inherent method dct_grid_to_reversible_dwt53_batch that just calls self.dispatch_reversible_dwt53_batch, duplicating the trait-impl method of the same name (both bodies identical); callers pick the inherent one only by name-resolution accident. Separately, buffer_with_slice is a one-line pass-through to j2k_metal_support::shared_buffer_with_slice.
evidence: lib.rs:595-600 body `self.dispatch_reversible_dwt53_batch(jobs)` equals trait method lib.rs:704-709; metal.rs:2409-2411 `buffer_with_slice` just calls `shared_buffer_with_slice`.
reduction: Drop the inherent method and let callers use the trait method (import the trait), and replace buffer_with_slice call sites with shared_buffer_with_slice directly.

title: Compact vs non-compact resident-encode paths are near-identical twins
location: crates/j2k-transcode-cuda/src/cuda.rs: device_band_groups_to_preencoded_components(1316-1416) vs device_band_groups_to_compact_preencoded_components(1419-1524); encode_resident_subbands(1156-1231) vs encode_resident_compact_subbands(1233-1313); split_resident_subband_blocks(1621-1676) vs split_resident_compact_subband_blocks(1678-1736); assemble_preencoded_components(1077-1113) vs assemble_compact_preencoded_components(1116-1154); device_bands_to_preencoded_components(920-948) vs device_bands_to_compact_preencoded_batch(951-998)
description: Every resident HT-encode helper exists in two copies that differ only in the output element type (PreencodedHtj2k97Subband/CodeBlock/Component vs their Compact* variants) and the runtime encode call (encode_htj2k_codeblocks_multi_resident_with_resources_and_pool vs ..._compact_...). The plan/target-building and validation logic is shared verbatim.
evidence: `diff <(sed -n '1323,1379p') <(sed -n '1426,1482p')` and `diff <(sed -n '1164,1206p') <(sed -n '1241,1283p')` both return EXIT=0 (byte-identical group_plans+targets blocks). assemble_* twins diff shows only type-name / mismatch-string differences. split_* twins share identical loop/shape-count/error structure differing only in CudaHtj2kEncodedCodeBlock vs CudaHtj2kCompactEncodedCodeBlock and into_parts() shape. The file already demonstrates the abstraction pattern (Htj2k97ComponentJob trait, dispatch_*_with_sink) that could absorb the output-type difference.
reduction: Extract the two verified-identical plan/target-building blocks into one helper; abstract the differing output block/subband type behind a trait (like the existing Htj2k97ComponentJob) or generic sink so each twin pair collapses to a single generic function. Removes ~150 LOC.
verify_note: Verified in crates/j2k-transcode-cuda/src/cuda.rs. `diff` of lines 1323-1379 vs 1426-1482 and 1164-1206 vs 1241-1283 both return EXIT=0 (byte-identical plan/target-building blocks), as claimed. The five twin pairs differ only in: output type (PreencodedHtj2k97Subband/Component vs *Compact* variants), the context method (encode_htj2k_codeblocks_multi_resident... vs ..._compact_...), and compact's extra `payload` return via into_payload_and_code_blocks(). The split_* twins (1621-1676 vs 1678-1736) share identical loop/shape-count/error structure differing only in CudaHtj2kEncodedCodeBlock (data field) vs CudaHtj2kCompactEncodedCodeBlock (payload_range) and into_parts() shape. assemble_* twins (1077-1113 vs 1116-1154) differ only in type names and error strings. These differences are cleanly abstractable behind a trait/generic sink (the file already uses Htj2k97ComponentJob generics), so consolidation is behavior-preserving within the single CUDA backend. Total duplicated body across the 5 pairs exceeds 300 LOC, so a net ~150 LOC saving is realistic even after trait machinery overhead.

title: Accelerator scaffold duplicated verbatim with j2k-transcode-metal
location: crates/j2k-transcode-cuda/src/lib.rs:99-303 (fields + accessors + CudaDispatchMode + recover/unavailable) vs crates/j2k-transcode-metal/src/lib.rs:319-651 (516-582 accessors, enum MetalDispatchMode:346)
description: The 12 attempts/dispatches counter fields, their 12 pub const accessor methods, the two-variant dispatch-mode enum, and the Auto/Explicit recover/unavailable helpers are structurally identical between the CUDA and Metal accelerators (same method names verified: reversible_dwt53_attempts..htj2k97_codeblock_batch_dispatches).
evidence: rg shows both crates define the identical accessor set (metal lib.rs:516-582 lists the same 12 names as cuda lib.rs:213-281); both define a two-variant *DispatchMode enum. Both crates depend on j2k-transcode, which owns the DctToWaveletStageAccelerator trait and could host a shared counter/dispatch-mode helper.
reduction: Hoist the counter bag + accessors + DispatchMode + recover/unavailable into a shared struct in j2k-transcode (or a tiny shared crate) that both accelerators embed. Saves ~100 LOC here (and a mirror amount in the Metal crate).
verify_note: Genuine cross-crate duplication confirmed. CUDA lib.rs:104-115 and Metal lib.rs:330-337 declare the identical 12 counter fields (reversible_dwt53/_batch, dwt53, dwt97/_batch, htj2k97_codeblock_batch attempts+dispatches) plus last_dwt97_batch_stage_timings. The 12 pub const accessors (CUDA 211-281 vs Metal 516-584) are byte-equivalent apart from doc-comment wording ('on the GPU' vs 'by Metal'). Both define a structurally identical two-variant DispatchMode enum (CudaDispatchMode / MetalDispatchMode, Explicit+Auto). Both crates depend on j2k-transcode which owns DctToWaveletStageAccelerator, so a shared counter struct (embedded + exposed via Deref to preserve the pub accessor API used by tests) is feasible and behavior-preserving. Caveat: the threshold/config fields differ (CUDA has 5 min_auto_* fields, Metal has 7) and recover()/unavailable() depend on backend-specific error types, so those are NOT trivially shareable and were correctly excluded from the shared scope by the finding. The ~100 LOC estimate for the CUDA crate is achievable only if accessors are fully hoisted (12 fields + 12 inits + ~72 accessor lines + enum); it is somewhat optimistic given the added Deref/shared-struct machinery and the cross-crate invasiveness, so ~90 is a more defensible figure.

title: Repetitive resident-batch trait-method dispatch boilerplate in lib.rs
description: The six preencoded/compact trait methods repeat the same skeleton: resident_ht_encode guard, attempts/counter increments, empty-input early return, Auto min-jobs/min-samples threshold check, cfg(not(cuda-runtime)) unavailable arm, cfg(cuda-runtime) dispatch+count arm.
evidence: Lines 576-593, 621-639, 667-688, 720-740, 774-797 are the same guard/threshold pattern with only the dispatch fn and empty-value differing (mirrors how the cuda.rs side already factored dispatch_htj2k97_preencoded_i16_batch_with_sink). The counter-update block Ok((output,timings)) => {...saturating_add...} is copy-pasted 6+ times.
reduction: Factor the guard+threshold+counter update into a private helper taking a closure for the actual cuda:: dispatch (parallel to the cuda.rs _with_sink helper). Saves ~60 LOC.

title: Manual field-by-field timing struct copies could be macro/helper-reduced
description: ~110 lines of hand-written field-by-field assignment/saturating_add across four functions mapping and accumulating Dwt97BatchStageTimings. accumulate_batch_timings alone lists ~20 fields each with `.saturating_add`, and set_/add_ht_encode_timings overlap heavily.
evidence: accumulate_batch_timings (264-312) is 20+ near-identical `total.X = total.X.saturating_add(next.X)` lines; add_ht_encode_timings (241-262) duplicates the ht_* subset of that logic.
reduction: Introduce a small saturating-add macro over the field list, or add accumulate/add helpers on Dwt97BatchStageTimings in j2k-transcode so all callers share one impl. Saves ~40 LOC.

title: Inline QuantizeParams construction duplicates htj2k97_quantize_params
description: dispatch_htj2k97_codeblock_batch builds CudaHtj2k97QuantizeParams inline with the same inv_delta closure and field mapping as the htj2k97_quantize_params helper used by every other resident path.
evidence: Lines 557-566 replicate lines 907-916 (identical inv_delta lambda and inv_delta_ll/hl/lh/hh + cb_width/cb_height assignment). The only reason it is inlined is that cb_width/cb_height were already destructured at line 535.
reduction: Call htj2k97_quantize_params(options) and reuse (cb_width,cb_height) from it. Saves ~10 LOC and removes a drift risk.
verify_note: Confirmed in crates/j2k-transcode-cuda/src/cuda.rs. Lines 557-566 (inside dispatch_htj2k97_codeblock_batch) replicate the inv_delta lambda and inv_delta_ll/hl/lh/hh + cb_width/cb_height assignment of htj2k97_quantize_params (907-916) verbatim. cb_width/cb_height (destructured at line 535 via validate_htj2k97_codeblock_options) are used ONLY at lines 564-565 within this function, so the call site can be replaced with `let params = htj2k97_quantize_params(options)?;`, dropping the separate validate call at 535 and the inline block at 557-566. That removes ~10-12 LOC and eliminates drift risk. Safe and sound: htj2k97_quantize_params performs the same validate_htj2k97_codeblock_options internally, so behavior is identical.

title: DecodeRequest + four *_request dispatch methods are unused (superseded by per-crate request enums)
description: DecodeRequest and its normalized full/ROI/scaled dispatch default methods form an abstraction that no production code uses. Every adapter crate rolls its own request enum instead: j2k::adapter::device_plan::DeviceDecodeRequest, j2k-jpeg JpegDecodeRequest, j2k-compare ExternalDecodeRequest. The DecodeRequest constructors (FULL/full/region/scaled/region_scaled/is_full_resolution_full_image/decoded_rect) and the four trait methods are pure dead weight; decoded_rect even reimplements the same roi/scale normalization that device_plan does.
evidence: rg for callers of decode_request_into|submit_request_to_device|decode_tile_request|submit_tile_request_to_device across crates/ returns only the `fn` definitions in traits.rs (0 call sites, incl tests/benches). `rg -w DecodeRequest` matches only its definition in types.rs, its re-export in lib.rs, its use as a param inside those same 4 default methods in traits.rs, and two references in crates/j2k-core/tests/api.rs. No other crate names j2k_core::DecodeRequest.
reduction: Delete the four *_request default trait methods and the DecodeRequest type (and its test-only exercises), or, if the public type must be kept for SemVer, at minimum drop the four unused default methods.
verify_note: rg across crates/ and xtask confirms zero call sites for decode_request_into (traits.rs:178), submit_request_to_device (:243), decode_tile_request (:407), submit_tile_request_to_device (:623). DecodeRequest (types.rs:118) is referenced only as the parameter of these 4 default methods plus tests/api.rs (lines 382,392); it is NOT re-exported by the top-level j2k facade crate (crates/j2k/src/lib.rs:83-88 does not list it), so it is not part of the top-level public API. Each method carries a real default body (~17 lines), so deletion yields genuine savings. Two of the methods sit on ImageDecode/TileBatchDecode traits that the facade re-exports, but they are effectively unreachable via the facade because their DecodeRequest argument is not exported there. Removal is safe within the workspace; the only caveat is j2k-core's own standalone SemVer, which the finding already flags. est_loc_saved ~145 is reasonable.

title: Exported enums BackendFailureKind and WarningKind are never referenced anywhere
description: Both enums are defined and re-exported from lib.rs but have zero uses in the entire workspace, including this crate's own src and tests. DecodeOutcome carries a generic warning type parameter W (adapters use their own warning enums), so WarningKind is orphaned; BackendFailureKind's intended 'map before codec-specific error' role was never wired up.
evidence: `rg -w BackendFailureKind crates/` -> only accelerator.rs definition + lib.rs re-export. `rg -w WarningKind crates/` -> only types.rs definition + lib.rs re-export. No constructors, matches, or impls anywhere.
reduction: Remove both enums and their pub re-exports in lib.rs.
verify_note: rg -w BackendFailureKind yields only the definition (accelerator.rs:120) and the internal lib.rs:43 re-export; rg -w WarningKind yields only the definition (types.rs:201) and lib.rs:75 re-export. No constructors, match arms, or impls exist. Neither enum is re-exported by the top-level j2k facade crate (crates/j2k/src/lib.rs:83-88), so they are not top-level public API. Removal of both enums and their re-exports is safe. est_loc_saved ~32 is about right (accelerator.rs 118-135 ~18 lines, types.rs 198-208 ~11 lines, plus re-export cleanup).

title: Redundant PassthroughRequirements component setters (with_components vs with_component_count)
description: Two builder methods set the same `components: Option<u16>` field with different integer widths (with_components(u8) and with_component_count(u16)). This is a small API smell: callers must know which width to use, and the u8 variant silently widens. One typed setter (u16) plus a doc note would suffice.
evidence: with_components (u8, line 233) and with_component_count (u16, line 240) both assign self.components. External callers: with_components used in ~2 sites, with_component_count in ~2 sites.
reduction: Collapse to a single with_components(u16) setter and migrate the two u8 call sites.

title: Internal-only buffer helpers exported as pub
description: ensure_allocation_within_cap and strided_output_len are pub and re-exported at the crate root but are only used inside buffer.rs (by strided_output_len_capped / validate_strided_output_buffer) and this crate's tests. They widen the public contract surface without any external consumer.
evidence: `rg -w ensure_allocation_within_cap` and `rg -w strided_output_len` across crates/ (excluding j2k-core) return 0 hits; only internal buffer.rs usage and j2k-core/tests/api.rs.
reduction: Demote both to pub(crate) (or private) and drop from the lib.rs re-export list; keep only strided_output_len_capped / validate_strided_output_buffer public.

title: Module sprawl: several trivially small single-purpose files
description: 16 modules for ~2.4k LOC, with five files under ~40 LOC each holding a single tiny trait/enum/fn. This is not harmful but adds navigation overhead; e.g. device.rs is one const fn, scratch.rs is one 2-method trait, row_sink.rs is one 1-method trait. They could be folded into related modules (e.g. row_sink+sample, device into backend) without losing cohesion.
evidence: wc -l: scratch.rs=9, row_sink.rs=12, device.rs=13, sample.rs=29, scale.rs=38. device.rs holds only validate_cuda_surface_backend_request; scratch.rs only the ScratchPool trait.
reduction: Merge the micro-modules into their nearest cohesive neighbor (device->backend, scratch->traits or backend, row_sink->sample) to cut module count and mod declarations.

title: Plan-building match cloned across decode_owned_cuda_rgb8 and decode_owned_cuda_rgb8_into
description: decode_owned_cuda_rgb8 and decode_owned_cuda_rgb8_into contain an identical 20-line match on OwnedFastRgb8Packet that calls cuda_decode_plan with the matching CudaJpegRgb8Sampling for each of the three variants. The two copies can drift independently.
evidence: Lines 55-74 (in decode_owned_cuda_rgb8) and 111-130 (in decode_owned_cuda_rgb8_into) are byte-identical: `let plan = match &packet { OwnedFastRgb8Packet::Fast420(packet) => cuda_decode_plan(CudaJpegRgb8Sampling::Fast420, ...), Fast422 => ..., Fast444 => ... };`
reduction: Add a private helper fn owned_cuda_decode_plan(packet: &OwnedFastRgb8Packet, dimensions, checkpoints) -> Result<CudaJpegRgb8DecodePlan> and call it from both functions.
verify_note: Lines 55-74 and 111-130 in owned_decode.rs are byte-identical match blocks (OwnedFastRgb8Packet -> cuda_decode_plan with Fast420/422/444 arms), both operating on the same &packet/dimensions/&checkpoints inputs and both under #[cfg(feature = "cuda-runtime")]. Extracting a private helper returning Result<CudaJpegRgb8DecodePlan> is safe and behavior-preserving; lifetimes borrow from packet+checkpoints which works. Real net savings are closer to ~12 (a ~20-line block appears twice, replaced by two one-line calls plus a ~26-line helper), so 18 is modestly inflated.

title: 77-line FastRgb8Packet trait + macro abstract three packets whose fields are already public
description: FastRgb8Packet declares 12 accessor methods and impl_fast_rgb8_packet! generates trivial `self.field` forwarders for JpegFast420/422/444PacketV1, solely so cuda_decode_plan can be generic over the three types. Because the macro accesses fields directly (self.mcus_per_row, self.entropy_bytes, self.y_quant, ...), those fields are already public and the trait adds no encapsulation; alongside the separate OwnedFastRgb8Packet enum (dimensions()/entropy_checkpoints() at 189-203) this is ~90 lines of dispatch boilerplate for three variants.
evidence: trait FastRgb8Packet at 207-220; macro body at 223-275 forwards each field verbatim; three impl_fast_rgb8_packet! invocations at 278-282; only consumer is cuda_decode_plan at 341-364.
reduction: Drop the trait+macro and have cuda_decode_plan (or a build_plan! macro) match on OwnedFastRgb8Packet directly and read the public packet fields, collapsing ~60 LOC of forwarders into one 3-arm match/macro.

title: Six copies of the same CUDA region/scaled rejection guard
description: The region, scaled, and region-scaled surface impls in both codec.rs and decoder.rs each begin with the same `if backend == BackendRequest::Cuda { return Err(Error::UnsupportedCudaRequest { reason: "J2K CUDA JPEG owned decode does not support <op> output" }); }` guard, repeated six times with only the op word changing.
evidence: rg 'does not support (region|scaled)' returns 6 matches across codec.rs:178/208/244 and decoder.rs:107/130/154, each preceded by the identical `if backend == BackendRequest::Cuda` check.
reduction: Introduce a small helper e.g. reject_cuda_non_full(backend, op) -> Result<()> returning the UnsupportedCudaRequest error, called once at the top of each of the six impls.
verify_note: rg confirms six identical guards: codec.rs:176-180/206-210/242-246 and decoder.rs:105-109/128-132/152-156, each immediately after validate_surface_request(backend)? and each returning Error::UnsupportedCudaRequest with one of three distinct reason strings (region / scaled / scaled region). A helper reject_cuda_non_full(backend, op)->Result<()> (or taking the reason &'static str) collapses each 5-line guard to one call and is behavior-preserving. ~15 LOC is a reasonable estimate.

title: MetricUnit is write-only: stored but never read
description: MetricUnit is threaded through ProfileField::metric / metric_with_summary and stored in ProfileFieldKind::Metric { unit, .. }, but no code ever reads the unit. summarize_metric() matches with `..` and ignores it, and there is no getter. The enum (5 variants + Other(String)) and the required `unit` parameter exist purely to be discarded, complicating three public constructors and 3 external call sites.
evidence: rg for `unit` in crates/j2k-profile/src shows only writes (field.rs:30,56,66,69); no read/match. summarize_metric() (field.rs:95-103) only inspects `summarize`. External uses only construct values (MetricUnit::Count/Bytes/Microseconds); none read them back.
reduction: Drop MetricUnit and the unit field; collapse ProfileFieldKind::Metric to just `{ summarize: bool }` (or a bool flag). Change metric()/metric_with_summary() to not take a unit. Simplifies public signatures and removes ~20 LOC.
verify_note: The unit field is only written (field.rs:56 and 69) and never read or matched anywhere in crates or xtask; summarize_metric() (field.rs:95-103) inspects only summarize, and no getter exposes unit. All 13 external references (j2k-jpeg-metal, j2k-cuda, j2k-jpeg-cuda) merely construct MetricUnit::{Count,Bytes,Microseconds} to pass in. Since j2k-profile is a #![doc(hidden)] internal crate with all callers inside the workspace, removing the enum + field + unit params is safe. Estimate is slightly low: enum (~16 lines) + field/signature changes plus the 13 argument lines shed at call sites push net savings higher than 22.

title: flush_profile_summary_to + ProfileSummary::flush_to are unused
description: The public helper flush_profile_summary_to has zero callers anywhere in the workspace, and the only caller of ProfileSummary::flush_to(sink) is that dead helper. Both are dead. (flush_to_stderr, used by Drop, is separate and live.)
evidence: rg 'flush_profile_summary_to' across crates (excluding j2k-profile) = 0 hits. rg '\.flush_to\(' across crates excluding summary.rs = only emit.rs:111 (the dead helper).
reduction: Delete flush_profile_summary_to (emit.rs) and ProfileSummary::flush_to (summary.rs) plus the lib.rs re-export.
verify_note: rg across crates+xtask returns 0 external hits for flush_profile_summary_to, and .flush_to( is only called from emit.rs:111 inside the dead helper itself. No test references either. Both are removable along with the lib.rs re-export; ~14 LOC is accurate (emit.rs 106-112 = 7, summary.rs 100-105 = 6, plus re-export).

title: emit_gpu_route_profile export is unused
description: emit_gpu_route_profile (the untyped key/value variant) has no callers; consumers use emit_gpu_route_fields, emit_gpu_route_surface_profile, and emit_gpu_route_decision_profile instead.
evidence: rg 'emit_gpu_route_profile\b' across crates excluding j2k-profile = 0 hits (emit_gpu_route_surface_profile/decision_profile match separately and are used).
reduction: Remove emit_gpu_route_profile and its re-export.
verify_note: rg for emit_gpu_route_profile with a word boundary returns 0 hits across crates+xtask; the used variants are emit_gpu_route_surface_profile, emit_gpu_route_decision_profile, and emit_gpu_route_fields (confirmed in j2k-cuda, j2k-jpeg-cuda, j2k-metal, j2k-jpeg-metal). The function (gpu_route.rs:97-112) is not called internally or in tests, so it and its re-export are safely removable. ~15 LOC accurate.

title: ProfileField::raw constructor is unused
description: ProfileField::raw (a thin alias for label) is never called in the crate or anywhere in the workspace.
evidence: rg 'ProfileField::raw|\.raw\(' across crates = 0 hits outside its definition (field.rs:73).
reduction: Delete the raw() method.
verify_note: rg for ProfileField::raw and .raw( returns no hits anywhere in crates or xtask (including tests). The method (field.rs:72-75, 4 lines including doc comment) just delegates to label() and is safely deletable.

title: Four emit_* helpers repeat the same ProfileStageMode match skeleton
description: All four public emitters share an identical `match mode { Disabled => {}, Rows => eprintln!(format...), Summary => summary.with(|s| s.borrow_mut().record_*) }` structure, differing only in the format fn and record method. The Disabled/Rows arms are near-verbatim copies four times.
evidence: emit.rs:35-48, 61-77, 88-104, 124-142 are structurally identical three-arm matches over ProfileStageMode.
reduction: Extract a small generic/closure-based dispatcher (e.g. a helper taking a format closure and a record closure) so each public fn is a one-line delegation; removes ~3 repeated match bodies.
verify_note: emit_profile_row (35-48), emit_profile_row_u128 (61-76), emit_profile_fields (88-103), and emit_profile_row_with_timing_summary (124-141) all use the identical 3-arm ProfileStageMode match (Disabled no-op; Rows -> eprintln of a format fn; Summary -> summary.with borrow_mut record call). A generic dispatcher taking on_rows: FnOnce() and on_summary: FnOnce(&mut ProfileSummary) cleanly factors the skeleton and is behavior-preserving (each arm runs at most once). Savings estimate is reasonable, arguably conservative.

title: record_str/record_u128/record_fields share a copy-pasted collect+entry tail
description: The three record_* methods each build a numeric_fields Vec guarded by `numeric_mode == Aggregate`, then run the identical `let row = self.rows.entry(key).or_default(); row.count = row.count.saturating_add(1); for (k,v) in numeric_fields { row.record_numeric(...) }` tail. Only the numeric extraction differs.
evidence: summary.rs:137-142, 166-171, 195-200 are identical blocks; the guarded numeric-collection loops at 124-135/155-164/183-193 differ only in value source.
reduction: Factor the shared tail into a private `commit(&mut self, key, numeric_fields)` helper (and optionally a numeric-extraction helper) called by all three record_* methods.
verify_note: The 5-line commit tail (summary.rs 137-141, 166-170, 195-199: entry/or_default, count saturating_add, record_numeric loop) is byte-identical across all three methods and is factorable into a private commit(&mut self, key, numeric_fields) helper. However the guarded numeric-collection loops (124-135/155-164/183-193) differ in iteration and value extraction (str parse vs raw u128 vs summarize_metric+parse) and are not cleanly mergeable, contrary to the inflated estimate. Factoring only the identical tail replaces 3x5=15 lines with 3 one-line calls plus a ~7-line helper, netting ~5 LOC, not 18.

title: gpu_route caching + field-builder helpers duplicate existing patterns
description: gpu_route_profile_stage_mode() reimplements the OnceLock<ProfileStageMode>-from-env caching that StageModeCache (env.rs:55-72) already provides. Separately, gpu_route_decision_fields and gpu_route_surface_fields are the same builder differing only by two width/height fields, and find_str_field/find_u128_field (summary.rs) differ only in how the value is stringified.
evidence: gpu_route.rs:18-21 hand-rolls `static MODE: OnceLock` + get_or_init, identical to StageModeCache::mode_from_env. gpu_route_decision_fields (44-61) is gpu_route_surface_fields (64-84) minus width/height. find_str_field/find_u128_field (summary.rs:359-382) share the find-by-key body.
reduction: Use StageModeCache inside gpu_route; have decision_fields delegate to a shared builder; unify the two find_* helpers via a value-formatting closure.
verify_note: All three sub-claims hold. (a) gpu_route.rs:18-21 hand-rolls static OnceLock + get_or_init, duplicating StageModeCache::mode_from_env (env.rs:69-71); swapping to StageModeCache is equivalent but yields ~0 LOC (consistency only). (b) gpu_route_decision_fields (44-61) is exactly gpu_route_surface_fields (64-84) with width/height inserted between fmt and decision; a shared builder taking Option<(u32,u32)> preserves field order and behavior, saving ~18 LOC. (c) find_str_field/find_u128_field (359-382) share the find-by-key body and differ only in value formatting, unifiable via a value-to-String closure (~8 LOC). Net across (b)+(c) supports the ~20 estimate; the reduction is safe.

title: J2kForwardDwt53Output/Level are byte-identical to J2kForwardDwt97Output/Level
description: The 5/3 and 9/7 forward-DWT output and detail-level structs are field-for-field identical: both *Output have {ll, ll_width, ll_height, levels: Vec<*Level>} and both *Level have the same 9 fields (hl, lh, hh, width, height, low_width, low_height, high_width, high_height). Only the type name differs (semantic 5/3 vs 9/7). They could be a single J2kForwardDwtOutput/J2kForwardDwtLevel pair (optionally aliased) since the data layout carries no 5/3-vs-9/7 distinction.
evidence: Read of lib.rs:141-224 shows identical field sets. J2kForwardDwt53Level (10 refs) and J2kForwardDwt97Level (9 refs) are used only as plain data; no trait impls differentiate them (no `impl` blocks exist in the crate for either).
reduction: Merge into one J2kForwardDwtOutput + J2kForwardDwtLevel type (keep 5/3 and 9/7 as type aliases if callers need distinct names), deleting the duplicate 97 definitions.
verify_note: lib.rs:141-224 confirms J2kForwardDwt53Output (145-152) and J2kForwardDwt97Output (192-201) have identical field sets, and J2kForwardDwt53Level (156-175) matches J2kForwardDwt97Level (205-224) field-for-field. Both store Vec<f32> (5/3 is nominally integer/reversible, 9/7 float, but the structs carry f32 in both cases), so they are structurally interchangeable. No impls exist in j2k-types (only #[derive(Debug, Clone)]). Caveat: the evidence's claim 'no impl blocks exist' is true only for the j2k-types crate; there are two byte-identical external impls of PrecomputedDwtGeometryView in j2k-native/src/j2c/encode.rs:2350 and :2383. Merging the two types (even via type aliases to one concrete type) would make those two impls conflict (E0119), so one impl must also be deleted -- which is trivial (identical bodies) and saves ~32 additional lines beyond the types file. The 35 LOC estimate for the types file is accurate.

title: PrecomputedHtj2k53* and PrecomputedHtj2k97* differ only in one field type
description: PrecomputedHtj2k53Component and PrecomputedHtj2k97Component both have {x_rsiz, y_rsiz, dwt}, differing only in dwt: J2kForwardDwt53Output vs J2kForwardDwt97Output. The two Image structs {width, height, bit_depth, signed, components} are identical modulo the component element type. If the DWT output types are unified (see prior finding), these Precomputed 53/97 pairs collapse into one generic (e.g. PrecomputedHtj2kComponent/Image) or a single type entirely.
evidence: Read of lib.rs:539-589; only the `dwt` field type differs. PrecomputedHtj2k53Image (8 refs) / 97Image (7 refs) used as plain data.
reduction: Genericize as PrecomputedHtj2kComponent<Dwt>/Image<...> or, after unifying DWT outputs, delete one 53/97 copy.
verify_note: lib.rs:539-589 confirms PrecomputedHtj2k53Component vs 97Component differ only in the dwt field type (J2kForwardDwt53Output vs J2kForwardDwt97Output), and the Image structs are field-identical (width/height/bit_depth/signed/components). Verified no impl blocks exist for any of these four types anywhere in crates/xtask -- they are plain #[derive(Debug, Clone)] data. Genericizing as PrecomputedHtj2kComponent<Dwt>/Image, or deleting one copy after unifying the DWT outputs (finding 1), is behavior-preserving. Deleting 97Component (~10 lines) + 97Image (~14 lines) = 24, matching the estimate.

title: Prequantized/Preencoded/PreencodedCompact hierarchies repeat the same 4-level skeleton three times
description: Three parallel HTJ2K image hierarchies each define Image/Component/Resolution/Subband(/CodeBlock) with near-identical skeletons: every *Resolution is {subbands: Vec<*Subband>}; every *Component is {x_rsiz, y_rsiz, resolutions: Vec<*Resolution>}; every *Subband is {sub_band_type, num_cbs_x, num_cbs_y, total_bitplanes, code_blocks: Vec<*CodeBlock>}; every *Image is {width, height, bit_depth, signed, components: Vec<*Component>} (Compact adds `payload`). Only the leaf CodeBlock structs (and Compact's payload) genuinely differ. This is ~15 structs where a generic Htj2kComponent<R>/Htj2kResolution<S>/Htj2kSubband<CB> (leaf CodeBlock kept concrete) would remove most repetition.
evidence: Read of lib.rs:591-777 shows identical intermediate-level field sets across the three families; all three are actively constructed in non-test code (PrequantizedHtj2k97Image built at j2k-transcode/htj2k97_codeblock_oracle.rs:365 and jpeg_to_htj2k.rs:2949; PreencodedHtj2k97Image at jpeg_to_htj2k.rs:2927; PreencodedHtj2k97CompactImage at jpeg_to_htj2k.rs:2904 and encode.rs:8633), so none are dead - the duplication is in the definitions.
reduction: Introduce generic intermediate types (Htj2kComponent<R>, Htj2kResolution<S>, Htj2kSubband<CB>) shared by all three stages, keeping only the distinct leaf CodeBlock types and Compact's payload field.
verify_note: lib.rs:591-777 confirms the three families share an identical Component/Resolution/Subband skeleton differing only in the leaf CodeBlock element type, plus PreencodedHtj2k97CompactImage's extra payload field. All are plain #[derive(Debug, Clone)] data structs with zero impl blocks (the only impls found -- DctToWaveletStageAccelerator for Prequantized97Accelerator/Preencoded97Accelerator in tests -- are on unrelated Accelerator types, not these data structs), so introducing generic Htj2kComponent<CB>/Resolution<CB>/Subband<CB> is safe and behavior-neutral across backends (data-only). Genuine duplication. My line count: removing 2 redundant copies each of Component (~10), Resolution (~6), Subband (~14) = ~60, plus deduping the identical Prequantized/Preencoded Image (~14) = ~74 net; the compact leaf and payload stay distinct. The 80 estimate is slightly inflated.

title: 777-line single lib.rs with ~50 flat top-level types (god-file)
description: All ~50 contract types (subband/codeblock, stage jobs, DWT outputs, packetization, precomputed/prequantized/preencoded hierarchies, dispatch report) live in one flat file. Grouping into modules (dwt, packetization, preencoded, jobs) would improve reviewability; note the crate doc intentionally states 'single file', so this is organizational rather than LOC-saving and net LOC change is roughly zero.
evidence: wc -l reports 777 lines in the only source file; Read shows no submodules and ~50 pub type declarations at one level.
reduction: Split into 3-4 focused modules re-exported from lib.rs; only worthwhile if pursued alongside the generic-consolidation findings above.

title: MetalDeviceSession type is entirely unused
description: The public MetalDeviceSession struct plus its new/system_default/device methods and hand-written Debug impl are never referenced by any consumer. All adapters acquire a raw metal::Device directly via system_default_device() and pass the Device around, never the session wrapper.
evidence: rg for 'MetalDeviceSession' across crates/*/{src,tests,benches,examples} excluding this crate returns 0 hits; not re-exported (no 'pub use j2k_metal_support' anywhere) and not used internally. Adapters instead import system_default_device (j2k-metal/src/lib.rs:65, j2k-jpeg-metal/src/lib.rs:48, j2k-transcode-metal/src/metal.rs:14).
reduction: Delete the MetalDeviceSession struct, its impl block, and the custom Debug impl.
verify_note: rg across all crates' src/tests/benches/examples shows MetalDeviceSession only in crates/j2k-metal-support/src/lib.rs (struct 235, impl 240, Debug impl 260). No re-export (no 'pub use j2k_metal_support'), no glob import, no internal use. Adapters use system_default_device directly (j2k-metal/src/lib.rs:65, j2k-jpeg-metal/src/lib.rs:48, j2k-transcode-metal/src/metal.rs). It is a pub item in an internal support crate that is not surfaced through any top-level public API. Deleting lines 232-266 (struct + impl + Debug impl) removes ~35 lines. est_loc_saved of 35 is accurate.

title: Unsafe buffer_contents_slice / buffer_contents_slice_mut wrappers are unused
description: The two public unsafe convenience wrappers buffer_contents_slice and buffer_contents_slice_mut (which just .expect() on the checked_ variants) are never called anywhere. Consumers use the checked_buffer_contents_slice API directly. Removing them also makes checked_buffer_contents_slice_mut test-only (see evidence), so that too could be gated or dropped.
evidence: rg for 'buffer_contents_slice'/'buffer_contents_slice_mut' outside this crate = 0 hits; used internally only by each other's dead callers. checked_buffer_contents_slice_mut has 0 external references (only j2k-transcode-metal uses the immutable checked_buffer_contents_slice at metal.rs:2547/2555). Only the immutable checked variant + shared_buffer_for_len are used externally.
reduction: Delete buffer_contents_slice and buffer_contents_slice_mut; consider making checked_buffer_contents_slice_mut #[cfg(test)] or removing it since nothing outside tests mutates via this API.
verify_note: rg confirms the unsafe wrappers buffer_contents_slice (lib.rs:406) and buffer_contents_slice_mut (lib.rs:423) are only referenced inside their own bodies (411, 428) which call the checked_* variants; zero external callers. Externally only checked_buffer_contents_slice is used (j2k-transcode-metal/src/metal.rs:2547/2555); checked_buffer_contents_slice_mut is used only in this crate's tests (594). Deleting the two unsafe wrappers is safe. However the estimate is inflated: buffer_contents_slice spans 398-413 (~16 lines incl cfg+doc) and buffer_contents_slice_mut spans 415-430 (~16 lines), totaling ~32 lines. The additional 'consider #[cfg(test)]' suggestion saves no production lines. Correcting to ~32.

title: j2k-jpeg-metal reimplements this crate's dispatch_1d/2d/3d_pipeline helpers
description: The whole point of j2k-metal-support is to share Metal dispatch boilerplate, yet j2k-jpeg-metal defines its own pub(super) dispatch_2d_pipeline, dispatch_3d_pipeline and dispatch_1d_pipeline whose bodies are near-identical to the support-crate versions (same thread_execution_width().max(1) / max_total_threads_per_threadgroup() height derivation). j2k-jpeg-metal even imports the support dispatch_2d_pipeline in viewport_cache.rs:7 while separately reimplementing it in kernel_helpers.rs, so the two coexist inconsistently in one crate.
evidence: kernel_helpers.rs:72-91 dispatch_2d_pipeline body matches lib.rs:484-496 (via two_d_threads_per_group); kernel_helpers.rs:95-116 dispatch_3d matches lib.rs:500-512; no import of dispatch_1d/3d from j2k_metal_support in j2k-jpeg-metal (rg of 'use j2k_metal_support' blocks shows only dispatch_2d_pipeline imported, in viewport_cache.rs).
reduction: Delete j2k-jpeg-metal's local dispatch_1d/2d/3d_pipeline and import them from j2k_metal_support (adjusting the 1d variant to the shared signature), consolidating on the support crate.
verify_note: Partially correct with an important caveat. dispatch_2d_pipeline (kernel_helpers.rs:72-92) and dispatch_3d_pipeline (94-115) ARE semantically equivalent to j2k-metal-support's versions (lib.rs:484-496 / 500-512 via two_d_threads_per_group): the only textual difference is jpeg-metal's extra .max(1) on height, which is redundant since max_threads is clamped >= width, so max_threads/width is always >=1. These two are genuine, safely-mergeable duplicates. BUT dispatch_1d_pipeline is NOT equivalent: jpeg-metal's (123-145) uses choose_1d_threadgroup_width (148-154) which rounds the requested thread count up to a SIMD multiple and clamps to min(max_threads,256), while the support crate's dispatch_1d_pipeline (465-473) simply uses one_d_threads_per_group(thread_execution_width()) ignoring thread count and max_total_threads. Consolidating 1d onto the shared signature would change threadgroup sizes and GPU dispatch/perf behavior, so that part is not a safe merge. Real savings are limited to removing 2d+3d (~41 lines) and importing them; the 1d variant and its choose_1d_threadgroup_width helper must stay. Correcting est_loc_saved from 45 to ~40.

title: shader_library, named_pipeline and mtl_size are pub but only used internally
description: shader_library and named_pipeline are exported as standalone public helpers but every consumer goes through MetalPipelineLoader (which calls them internally); mtl_size is public but only used inside this crate's own dispatch helpers. This widens the public surface with no external caller.
evidence: rg for 'shader_library' and 'named_pipeline' outside this crate = 0 hits; 'mtl_size' outside this crate = 0 hits. MetalPipelineLoader (lib.rs:522-533) is the only caller of shader_library/named_pipeline; mtl_size only called at lib.rs:447,460,470,479,489,505.
reduction: Demote shader_library, named_pipeline, and mtl_size to non-pub (module-private) unless a documented external helper role is intended.

title: expected_size() is a near-useless trait method (3 of 4 impls return Ok(None) ignoring input)
description: Three of the four TileDecompress impls implement expected_size as `fn expected_size(_input) { Ok(None) }`, i.e. they discard the argument and provide no information. Only UncompressedCodec returns a meaningful value. Since no workspace code calls expected_size at all, this is dead surface repeated across files; if the trait were pruned or given a defaulted method these boilerplate impls would vanish.
evidence: Identical `Ok(None)` bodies in deflate.rs:18-20, lzw.rs:18-20, zstd_codec.rs:17-19; no caller of `expected_size` exists (`rg 'expected_size' crates/` outside this crate returns only the trait def in j2k-core).
reduction: Provide a default `expected_size` returning Ok(None) on the j2k-core trait and delete the three boilerplate overrides (or drop the method if truly unused).

title: UncompressedCodec inlines the OutputTooSmall/copy pattern already provided by bounded.rs helpers
description: uncompressed.rs manually constructs `BufferError::OutputTooSmall { required, have }` and does `out[..input.len()].copy_from_slice(input)`, duplicating observed_too_small() and copy_scratch_to_output() from bounded.rs which express exactly these two operations.
evidence: uncompressed.rs:23-26 rebuilds `BufferError::OutputTooSmall` that bounded.rs:64-66 (observed_too_small) already wraps; uncompressed.rs:28 `out[..input.len()].copy_from_slice(input)` matches bounded.rs:69 (copy_scratch_to_output).
reduction: Reuse observed_too_small/copy_scratch_to_output in UncompressedCodec for consistency, removing the inlined variants.
verify_note: Verified genuine and safe. bounded.rs:64-66 observed_too_small(required, have) constructs exactly BufferError::OutputTooSmall { required, have }, and bounded.rs:68-71 copy_scratch_to_output(src, out) does out[..src.len()].copy_from_slice(src) returning src.len(). Both are pub(crate) so reachable from uncompressed.rs. uncompressed.rs:23-26 inlines the identical BufferError::OutputTooSmall { required: input.len(), have: out.len() } (matches observed_too_small(input.len(), out.len())) and line 28 out[..input.len()].copy_from_slice(input); Ok(input.len()) is exactly copy_scratch_to_output(input, out). Rewriting to reuse both helpers is behavior-identical (same error values, same copy, same return) and also lets the direct BufferError import (uncompressed.rs:4, used only here) be dropped. Net savings ~4-5 lines; est of 5 is accurate.

title: Transcode --htj2k/--lossless-53 flags are required-but-inert ceremony
description: parse_transcode_args tracks two booleans (htj2k, lossless_53) whose only effect is to return an error when the flag is absent (lines 69-74). Neither boolean is stored in TranscodeArgs or ever influences behavior: transcode_jpeg_to_htj2k_bytes unconditionally calls JpegToHtj2kOptions::lossless_53() (line 107). So the flags are mandatory yet functionally no-ops — pure ceremony that a user must type. The whole parser could collapse to positional input/output extraction (the two match arms at lines 63-64 plus the ok_or_else checks), dropping the htj2k/lossless_53 vars, the two `--flag` match arms (58-59), and the two validation blocks (69-74).
evidence: Line 107: `j2k_transcode::jpeg_to_htj2k(bytes, &j2k_transcode::JpegToHtj2kOptions::lossless_53())` is the only options construction and ignores parsed flags. Lines 53-54 declare `htj2k`/`lossless_53`; they are read only at lines 58-59 (set) and 69-74 (validate-presence). Confirmed JpegToHtj2kOptions::lossless_53() is also the Default (jpeg_to_htj2k.rs:196-198), so no alternative path exists in-crate.
reduction: Either wire the flags to real option variants (e.g. accept a --lossy-97 alternative) so they carry meaning, or drop the required flags and their two bools/validation blocks and parse input/output positionally. If keeping the flags for forward-compat CLI stability, add a one-line comment noting they are reserved; otherwise remove ~8 lines.

title: sanitize_id duplicated verbatim across two adoption modules
description: Two byte-identical implementations of sanitize_id (lowercase ascii-alnum, collapse runs to '_', trim) differ only in the empty-fallback literal ("fixture" vs "source").
evidence: rg 'fn sanitize_id' -> src/adoption_materialize.rs:276, src/adoption_curate.rs:367. Bodies read identically except the final fallback string. Both modules already share crate::adoption_corpus (materialize/manifest/curate all `use crate::adoption_corpus::...`), which is the natural home.
reduction: Move one copy into adoption_corpus with a `fallback: &str` param and call it from both modules.
verify_note: Confirmed both bodies at xtask/src/adoption_curate.rs:367-382 and xtask/src/adoption_materialize.rs:276-291 are byte-identical except the final fallback literal ("fixture" vs "source"). Both modules already `use crate::adoption_corpus::...` (curate.rs:12, materialize.rs:13), so adoption_corpus is a valid shared home. Moving to one fn with a `fallback: &str` param is behavior-preserving. Net: two ~16-line copies replaced by one ~17-line fn saves ~15 lines; estimate slightly high but essentially accurate.

title: cargo() env helper triplicated
description: Identical `fn cargo() -> OsString { env::var_os("CARGO").unwrap_or_else(|| OsString::from("cargo")) }` is defined three times.
evidence: rg 'fn cargo\(\)' -> three matches; confirmed the three bodies are line-for-line identical.
reduction: Expose one `pub(crate) fn cargo()` in process.rs and delete the other two definitions.
verify_note: Confirmed three line-for-line identical definitions: main.rs:1458, perf_guard.rs:766, adoption_benchmark.rs:2021, each `env::var_os("CARGO").unwrap_or_else(|| OsString::from("cargo"))`. process.rs already hosts the shared command helpers (run_command, command_output_os, etc., all pub(crate)), so exposing one pub(crate) cargo() there and deleting two copies is the natural, behavior-preserving consolidation. Removing 2 of 3 three-line fns saves ~6 lines.

title: render_report is a ~389-line god-function
description: render_report builds the entire markdown report inline (methodology, measured tables, criterion tables, metal decode/auto/resident/transcode sections) in one function, driving deep nesting and making the section helpers hard to test in isolation.
evidence: Function spans lines 728-1116 (389 lines measured via awk). It orchestrates many single-purpose helpers (measured_table, criterion_estimate_table, metal_decode_summary, metal_auto_summary, metal_resident_summary, metal_transcode_summary) but inlines the sequencing/branching for each.
reduction: Extract per-section render blocks into `render_*_section(out, ...)` helpers and have render_report just sequence them; collapses branching and enables targeted unit tests.
verify_note: Confirmed render_report spans xtask/src/adoption_report.rs:728-1114 (~386 lines), a real god-function that inlines section sequencing/branching (Bundle, Publication Gates, Methodology, CPU Decode Rows, plus metal_* summaries). Extracting per-section helpers is safe and improves testability, so the defect is real. However est_loc_saved of 40 is inflated: this is a pure extraction/reorganization refactor that relocates code rather than deleting it, and added helper signatures typically net near-zero LOC reduction. Correcting the estimate down.

title: write_readme and write_summary in adoption_benchmark are oversized string builders
description: Both functions assemble large multi-section outputs (README markdown / summary JSON+TSV) inline with repeated push_str/writeln and per-step branching, exceeding the crate's own clippy::too_many_lines baseline threshold.
evidence: Line spans measured: write_readme 1733->1941 = 208 lines; write_summary 804->984 = 180 lines. STRICT_CLIPPY_BASELINE_ALLOWED_LINTS in main.rs:271 explicitly allowlists clippy::too_many_lines, i.e. these are known-oversized and lint-suppressed.
reduction: Split each into per-section helper writers; reduces nesting and lets the too_many_lines allow be dropped later.

title: adoption_benchmark.rs and adoption_report.rs are god-files
description: Two files hold ~24% of the crate each, mixing CLI parsing, step execution, profile-line parsing, JSON/TSV assembly, markdown rendering, and publication-gate logic. This concentration is what forces the long functions and duplicated metal parsers above.
evidence: wc -l: adoption_benchmark.rs 2622, adoption_report.rs 2387 (largest files in crate). Both contain distinct concern clusters: benchmark has run_* step fns + parse_metal_* + write_summary/write_readme + gate logic; report has collect_required_* issue collectors + render_* + metal_* summaries.
reduction: Split each into submodules (e.g. adoption_benchmark::{steps, metal_parse, summary, readme} and adoption_report::{gates, render, metal}) to isolate concerns and make duplication reduction above tractable.

title: Redundant command-runner wrapper layer in main.rs over process module
description: Several main.rs free functions merely forward to the process module with no added logic: command_output_os -> process::command_output_os, command_output_allow_failure -> process::command_output_allow_failure, run_program -> process::run_command(...CommandContext::new().envs()). This is a thin duplicate indirection layer.
evidence: main.rs:1470-1472 command_output_os body is only `process::command_output_os(program, args)`; 1466-1468 command_output_allow_failure only forwards; 1439-1441 run_program only wraps process::run_command with an empty CommandContext.
reduction: Call process:: functions directly at the ~10 call sites (or keep only run_cargo/run_nightly_cargo which add real args) and delete the pass-through wrappers.

title: 9/7 irreversible DWT lifting coefficients duplicated across every backend
description: The Cohen-Daubechies-Feauveau 9/7 lifting constants ALPHA=-1.5861343, BETA=-0.052980117, GAMMA=0.8829111, DELTA=0.44350687, KAPPA=1.2301741 (and INV_KAPPA) are declared independently in at least 8 places across 6 crates. Native even declares them twice within idwt.rs (scalar path at 1067 and SIMD path at 1414). j2k-transcode uses the full-precision f64 forms of the same values.
evidence: fdwt.rs:15 `const ALPHA: f32 = -1.586_134_3;`; idwt.rs:1067 `const NEG_ALPHA: f32 = 1.586_134_3;`; dct97_2d.rs:13 `const ALPHA: f64 = -1.586_134_342_059_924;`; cuda idwt simt:9 `const IDWT_NEG_ALPHA: f32 = 1.5861343;`; cuda encode simt:11 `const J2K_FDWT97_ALPHA: f32 = -1.5861343;`; metal idwt.metal:456 `const float NEG_ALPHA = 1.5861343f;`; dct97.metal:84 `constant float DCT97_ALPHA = -1.586134342059924f;`. All five constants match to available precision in each site.
reduction: Add a shared no_std constants module (e.g. j2k-types::dwt or a new j2k-dwt-const crate) exposing the canonical f64 coefficients plus f32 aliases; have native fdwt/idwt (both scalar and SIMD), j2k-transcode dct97, and both cuda-oxide SIMT crates import them. Metal/CUDA shader text cannot import Rust consts directly, but the values can be injected via generated `#define`s from the same source so the .metal/.cu literals stop being hand-maintained copies.
verify_note: Real duplication confirmed. Full 9/7 coefficient sets (ALPHA/BETA/GAMMA/DELTA/KAPPA/INV_KAPPA) are re-declared in fdwt.rs:15-20, idwt.rs:1067-1072 and 1414-1420, dct97_2d.rs:13-18, cuda idwt simt:9-14, cuda encode simt:11-16, plus shader copies (metal fdwt:34-35 KAPPA only, metal idwt:456-461, transcode-metal dct97.metal:84-89). f32 sites truncate to 1.5861343 while f64 sites (dct97) keep full precision, but the f32 literals equal `f64_canonical as f32`, so a shared f64 module + f32 aliases is behavior-preserving. Rust-side duplicate const lines total ~36; the shader literals cannot import Rust consts and generating #defines adds build machinery that offsets the saving, so the 55 estimate overstates net reduction.

title: MCT color-transform coefficients and sample logic (ICT + RCT) copied across native/CUDA/Metal
description: The irreversible color transform (ICT/inverse ICT: Y=0.299R+0.587G+0.114B, R=Y+1.402Cr, G=Y-0.34413Cb-0.71414Cr, B=Y+1.772Cb) and the reversible RCT (Y=floor((R+2G+B)/4), Cb=B-G, Cr=R-G) are reimplemented with the same literal coefficients in the native scalar/SIMD path, the Metal shader, and both CUDA cuda-oxide SIMT kernels (forward in encode, inverse in decode_store).
evidence: native inverse mct.rs:174 `*y0 = src0 + 1.402 * src2;`/`*y1 = src0 - 0.34413*src1 - 0.71414*src2;` matches metal mct.metal:65-67 `plane0[gid] = y2 * 1.402f + y0 ...` and cuda decode_store:224-226 `src0 + 1.402 * src2, src0 - 0.34413*src1 - 0.71414*src2, src0 + 1.772*src1`. Forward ICT native forward_mct.rs:71 `0.299*r0+0.587*g0+0.114*b0` matches cuda encode simt:1191 `0.299 * r + 0.587 * g + 0.114 * b` and metal mct.metal:119. RCT native forward_mct.rs:37 `floor_f32((r0 + 2.0*g0 + b0)*0.25)` is byte-identical to metal mct.metal:99 `floor((r + 2.0f*g + b)*0.25f)`.
reduction: Move the ICT/RCT coefficients into the shared constants module and extract pure `inverse_mct_sample`/`forward_ict_sample`/`forward_rct_sample` reference functions (already exist inline in cuda decode_store as inverse_mct_sample) into a shared no_std helper reused by native and the cuda-oxide SIMT crates; drive the shader literals from the same constant source.
verify_note: Confirmed real duplication of ICT/RCT coefficients and sample math. Inverse ICT (1.402 / -0.34413 / -0.71414 / 1.772) appears in native mct.rs:154-176 (SIMD + scalar remainder), cuda decode_store inverse_mct_sample:221-229, and metal mct.metal:65-67; forward ICT (0.299/0.587/0.114) and RCT floor((r+2g+b)*0.25) match across forward_mct.rs, cuda encode:1191, metal:93,119. cuda decode_store already has a pure inverse_mct_sample. However native's hot path is SIMD f32x8, so only the scalar remainder could call a shared pure helper (SIMD stays), and Metal/CUDA shader text cannot import Rust; the truly dedup-able surface is the coefficient constants plus the cuda/native scalar helpers, so 45 is inflated.

title: HTJ2K SigProp neighbor spread-mask table (SIGPROP_SPREAD_MASKS[16]) copied 4x + inlined in Metal
description: The 16-entry significance-propagation spread mask table [0x33,0x76,0xEC,0xC8, 0x330,...,0xC8000] is declared identically in native HT decode and encode, and again in the CUDA cuda-oxide HT decode and encode SIMT kernels. Metal re-expresses the same four base masks (0x33/0x76/0xEC/0xC8) shifted inline.
evidence: All four Rust copies are the literal `[0x33, 0x76, 0xEC, 0xC8, 0x330, 0x760, 0xEC0, 0xC80, 0x3300, 0x7600, 0xEC00, 0xC800, 0x33000, 0x76000, 0xEC000, 0xC8000]`; consumers use `SIGPROP_SPREAD_MASKS[bit] & inv_sig & !processed` (ht_block_decode.rs:1377, cuda decode simt:282). Metal ht_cleanup.metal:785-818 builds `0x33u << i`, `0x76u << i`, `0xECu << i`, `0xC8u << i`.
reduction: Declare SIGPROP_SPREAD_MASKS once in a shared no_std ht-const module and import into native (decode+encode) and both cuda-oxide SIMT crates; generate the Metal shifts from the same base values.
verify_note: Verified: identical `const SIGPROP_SPREAD_MASKS: [u32;16]` literal appears in native ht_block_decode.rs:69-71, native ht_block_encode.rs:18-20, cuda decode simt:18-20, and cuda encode simt:26-28, all consumed as SIGPROP_SPREAD_MASKS[bit] & inv_sig & !processed. Metal ht_cleanup.metal:785-818 builds the same values via 0x33/0x76/0xEC/0xC8 << i. Deduping the 4 Rust copies (3 lines each) into one shared no_std const saves the 3 redundant copies (~9 lines) minus a couple of import lines; Metal cannot import. 15 slightly overstates the net.

title: HTJ2K MEL adaptive-run exponent table / state machine duplicated native vs CUDA
description: The MEL (adaptive run-length) exponent table [0,0,0,1,1,1,2,2,2,3,3,4,5] and its k-index advance/threshold state machine are reimplemented in native HT decode, native HT encode, and both CUDA cuda-oxide HT SIMT kernels.
evidence: cuda decode simt:293 `const MEL_EXP: [u32; 13] = [0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, 5];` with `let eval = MEL_EXP[decoder.k as usize];`. Native uses the same 13-entry table via a `const n` binding driving `self.k = (self.k+1).min(n.len()-1)` and `self.threshold = 1 << n[self.k]` (ht_block_encode.rs). CUDA encode expresses it as `fn n(k: u32) -> u32`.
reduction: Extract the MEL exponent table and the small MEL encoder/decoder state helpers into a shared no_std module used by native and the cuda-oxide SIMT crates.
verify_note: Confirmed. The MEL_EXP table [0,0,0,1,1,1,2,2,2,3,3,4,5] appears in native ht_block_decode.rs:845, native ht_block_encode.rs:13, and cuda decode simt:293 (cuda encode expresses it as a fn). The decode_more_runs/get_run state machine is the same algorithm in native (ht_block_decode.rs:844-878, Option/`?` idiom, ForwardBitReader methods) and cuda (simt:292-331, bool-return free fns on MelDecoder) — semantically identical run-length logic. It is mergeable but requires a trait abstraction over the differing bit-reader/error idioms, which reclaims some of the saved lines, so 30 is a bit high.

title: Forward 9/7 1D lifting loop near-identical between native fdwt and j2k-transcode dct97
description: The 1D forward 9/7 lifting routine (four alpha/beta/gamma/delta predict/update passes over odd/even samples with the same symmetric boundary extension, followed by INV_KAPPA/KAPPA scaling) is a line-for-line copy between native fdwt.rs and j2k-transcode dct97_2d.rs; only the element type (f32 vs f64) and the removed comments differ.
evidence: Both share the exact `let last_even = if n%2 {n-2} else {n-1};` boundary and identical passes: `for i in (1..n).step_by(2) { let left=data[i-1]; let right = if i+1<n {data[i+1]} else {data[last_even]}; data[i] += ALPHA*(left+right); }` etc., ending with `data[i] *= INV_KAPPA` / `data[i] *= KAPPA`. See fdwt.rs:451-491 vs dct97_2d.rs:334-371.
reduction: Make the lifting routine generic over a float trait (or f64 core with f32 wrapper) in a shared dwt module and have native fdwt and j2k-transcode dct97 call it; keeps the transcode f64 precision path while removing the duplicate loop body.
verify_note: Verified byte-for-byte: fdwt.rs:448-492 (forward_lift_97 over &mut [f32]) and dct97_2d.rs:332-372 (forward_lift_97 over &mut [f64]) share the identical last_even boundary and all six lifting/scale passes, differing only in float type and comments. A generic-over-float (or f64 core + f32 wrapper) merge preserves the transcode f64-precision path and removes the duplicate ~38-line body; net saving is close to the body size minus a small generic wrapper. 45 is marginally high.

title: 5/3 reversible DWT predict/update lifting reimplemented in native, CUDA, and Metal
description: The reversible 5/3 lifting (predict: high -= floor((left+right)/2)+? ; update: low += floor((left+right+2)/4)) and its parity/boundary handling are independently implemented as scalar Rust (native), SIMT Rust (cuda-oxide idwt), and Metal shader helpers. The rounding helpers ((value+1)>>1 and origin>>1 index mapping) are also duplicated.
evidence: cuda idwt simt:120 `(value + 1) >> 1` and :128 `(output_coord >> 1) - (output_origin >> 1)` mirror metal idwt.metal:69 `return (value + 1u) >> 1u;` and :77 `return (coord >> 1u) - (origin >> 1u);`. cuda `lift_53_sample`/`filter_step_horizontal_53` (160,169) correspond to metal `reversible53_predict`/`reversible53_update` (93,97) and the native 5/3 filter.
reduction: Extract the pure Rust 5/3 sample helpers and index/rounding helpers into a shared no_std dwt module reused by native and the cuda-oxide idwt SIMT crate; generate the Metal helper bodies from the same reference. The per-thread SIMT dispatch loops stay backend-specific.
verify_note: Confirmed real duplication of the f32 5/3 sample math and index/rounding helpers. Native reversible_filter_53r (idwt.rs:1005-1026) uses s - floor(mul_add(l+r,0.25,0.5)) / s + floor((l+r)*0.5); cuda lift_53_sample (simt:160-166) and metal reversible53_predict/update (idwt.metal:93-99) are identical. Index helpers div_ceil_2/idwt_band_coord/pse_left/pse_right (cuda:118-157) mirror metal ceil_div2_u32/low_index/high_index/periodic_symmetric_extension (idwt.metal:68-91). But native also carries i64 and SIMD variants that don't map to cuda's f32-only kernel, and Metal cannot import Rust; only the f32 scalar sample + index helpers are truly shareable between native and cuda, so 40 is inflated.

title: Entire HTJ2K block coder (cleanup/SigProp/MagRef + MEL/VLC) forked scalar (native) vs SIMT (cuda-oxide)
location: crates/j2k-native/src/j2c/ht_block_decode.rs (2316 loc) + ht_block_encode.rs (2145 loc); crates/j2k-cuda-runtime/src/cuda_oxide_htj2k_decode/simt/src/main.rs (1322 loc) + cuda_oxide_htj2k_encode/simt/src/main.rs (1996 loc)
description: The HTJ2K FBCOT block coder (cleanup pass quad significance, SigProp, MagRef refinement, MEL run coder, VLC/UVLC packing) is implemented twice: as native scalar Rust and again as CUDA cuda-oxide SIMT Rust. Beyond the shared constant tables already flagged (SIGPROP masks, MEL exponents), the pass structure, quad pattern constants (e.g. pattern=0x3333 at native decode:1323 and encode:551, cuda decode:729), and the VLC/UVLC decode arithmetic are same-shape reimplementations differing mainly in the thread/index model.
evidence: Both native and cuda decode compute `pattern = 0x3333` in the cleanup quad expansion (native ht_block_decode.rs:1323, cuda decode simt:729); both share SIGPROP_SPREAD_MASKS and MEL_EXP tables (see other findings); the VLC/UVLC lookup tables themselves are already correctly shared (uploaded from j2k-native into CUDA via CudaHtj2kEncodeTables and into Metal as buffer params), proving the surrounding logic is the remaining fork. ~4400 native loc vs ~3300 cuda loc cover the same coding stages.
reduction: Do not merge the SIMT dispatch with scalar loops (execution models differ, high risk). Instead extract the execution-model-independent pieces into a shared no_std ht-core module: the constant tables (masks, MEL exponents, quad patterns), the MEL/VLC/UVLC codeword math, and pure per-quad helpers; have native and cuda-oxide call these. This shrinks both sides without changing kernel launch structure.
verify_note: The described fork is real: native (ht_block_decode.rs ~2316 loc + ht_block_encode.rs ~2145) and cuda-oxide SIMT crates (decode ~1322 + encode ~1996) independently implement the same coding stages, and both share the 0x3333 quad pattern, SIGPROP_SPREAD_MASKS and MEL_EXP tables. However the 250-loc estimate is speculative and not verifiable: the finding itself (correctly) forbids merging the dispatch/loop structure since the execution models differ, the VLC/UVLC tables are already shared via upload, and the genuinely execution-model-independent surface largely overlaps findings 3, 4, and 8 (masks, MEL, coefficient_to_i32) which are individually small. Most of the ~4400 vs ~3300 loc is structure-specific and cannot be safely collapsed, so the magnitude of a safe reduction is much smaller and uncertain.

title: Dequantization step/mantissa-exponent math split between native and CUDA SIMT
description: The irreversible dequantization mapping (coefficient magnitude -> value using the step size delta and k_max) plus the sign/magnitude reconstruction is implemented in native quantize.rs and again in the CUDA cuda-oxide dequantize SIMT kernel. The native side computes the step Delta = 2^(Rb-exponent)*(1+mantissa/2048); the CUDA side consumes a precomputed dequantization_step and applies the same coefficient_to_float scaling, so the sign-magnitude and scaling logic overlaps.
evidence: native quantize.rs:60 `Delta = 2^(R_b - exponent) * (1 + mantissa/2048)`; cuda dequantize simt:72 `fn coefficient_to_i32(value, k_max)` and :83 `fn coefficient_to_float_bits(value, k_max, scale)` reproduce the sign/magnitude-to-value reconstruction the native path performs when applying dequant.
reduction: Extract the pure coefficient->value reconstruction (sign/magnitude + scale) into a shared no_std helper used by both native dequant and the cuda-oxide dequantize kernel; keep step-size computation native and pass the scalar step to the kernel (already done).
verify_note: The title and primary evidence are mis-cited: native quantize.rs:58-83 (delta/from_delta) is step-SIZE (mantissa/exponent) computation, which the reduction itself says to keep native and which has NO cuda duplicate (the scalar step is passed to the kernel). The genuinely duplicated piece is coefficient_to_i32: native ht_block_decode.rs:225-234 (`shift = 31.saturating_sub(k_max); (value & 0x7FFF_FFFF) >> shift`, sign from bit 31) is identical to cuda dequantize simt:72-80 and also cuda htj2k_decode simt:193 — 3 copies of a ~9-line pure reconstruction. Extracting that into a shared no_std helper is safe and sound, so the reduction's actual target is valid, but it is a small function (~9-18 lines reclaimable across copies), not the 25 claimed, and the step/mantissa-exponent framing is inaccurate.

title: Integer jidctint IDCT reimplemented in all three backends (plus CPU SIMD copies)
description: The libjpeg-turbo 'islow' integer IDCT (jidctint) is written out four+ times: the CPU scalar reference, two CPU SIMD ports (avx2/neon, self-documented as 'Bit-exact with super::scalar::idct_islow'), the CUDA kernel, and the Metal shader. All share the identical CONST_BITS=13/PASS1_BITS=2 fixed-point scheme, the same butterfly (z1=(z2+z3)*FIX_0_541196100; tmp2=z1-z3*FIX_1_847759065; tmp3=z1+z2*FIX_0_765366865; ...), the same DC-only early-out, and the same descale/rounding.
evidence: CPU scalar.rs:150-201 and CUDA main.rs:511-564 and Metal shaders.metal:1435-1478 are line-for-line the same expression tree with identical FIX_* operands; CPU idct/scalar.rs:21-32 defines FIX_0_298631336=2446..FIX_3_072711026=25172, CUDA main.rs:105-111 defines JPEG_FIX_0_298631336=2446.. identically, Metal shaders.metal:316+ defines FIX_0_298631336=2446 identically. Downscale 4x4/2x2 variants are also mirrored (CPU downscale.rs vs Metal idct_islow_4x4/2x2/1x1).
