reduction: Extract a #![no_std] scalar IDCT (full + 4x4/2x2/1x1 + dc-only) into a shared crate (e.g. j2k-jpeg-kernel-core) consumed by both j2k-jpeg scalar path and the CUDA SIMT kernel (both are Rust). For Metal, generate the FIX_* constant block from the same Rust source via build.rs and keep the MSL body as a documented port referencing the canonical implementation.
verify_note: Verified: CPU idct_1d_column (scalar.rs:125-202) and CUDA idct_column (main.rs:488-565) are the identical Chen-Wang expression tree with identical FIX_* constants (2446/4433/6270/9633/15137/16069/16819/20995/25172), and Metal shaders.metal:1408+ mirrors them; constants blocks match verbatim (scalar.rs:21-32 vs main.rs:105-116 vs metal:314+). Real, substantial duplication. The two Rust backends (CPU + CUDA SIMT) are genuinely mergeable via a no_std core crate. Caveats reducing the estimate: CPU uses core::num::Wrapping<i32> and is generic over u8/u16 (12-bit path) with a separate idct_1d_column_bottom_half_zero optimization, while the CUDA copy is plain i32/u8-only, so a shared version must reconcile these; Metal (MSL) cannot be merged and only its FIX_* constants can be codegen'd. Net realistic elimination is ~180-200 LOC (one CUDA copy removed into shared crate + constant blocks), not 260.

title: Canonical JPEG Huffman table build + decode duplicated across CPU/CUDA-host/Metal
location: j2k-jpeg/src/entropy/huffman.rs:64-136 (from_raw) and :202-333 (decode + packing); j2k-cuda-runtime/src/jpeg.rs:50-114 (host table build); j2k-cuda-runtime/src/cuda_oxide_jpeg_decode/simt/src/main.rs:352-411,915-1016 (decode_symbol_real + entropy scan); j2k-jpeg-metal/src/shaders.metal:1150-1300+ (prepare_huffman + decode)
description: The T.81 Annex C canonical code construction (build huffsize[] from bits[], derive huffcode[] by the code<<=1/code+=1 loop, then min_code/max_code/val_offset per length) and the subsequent decode-by-length lookup are implemented four times with the same variable structure (min_code[17]/max_code[17]/val_offset[17]).
evidence: CPU huffman.rs:79-121 (huffsize/huffcode loops, min_code[len]=huffcode[k], val_offset[len]=k-min_code[len]) is reproduced almost verbatim in CUDA jpeg.rs:50-114 (same huffsize/huffcode/max_code/val_offset build) and in Metal shaders.metal:1150-1200 (prepare_huffman: same huffsize/huffcode loop, min_code/max_code/val_offset). Decode-by-length (max_code>=0 && code<=max_code -> values[code+val_offset]) matches between huffman.rs decode and CUDA main.rs:326-340/366-380.
reduction: Move the canonical build into the shared no_std core crate; have CPU HuffmanTable::from_raw and the CUDA host jpeg.rs both call it (both are std/no_std Rust). Keep Metal's prepare_huffman as a documented port but drive its layout/field order from the shared struct definition to prevent divergence.
verify_note: The canonical BUILD is genuinely duplicated: CPU from_raw (huffman.rs:79-121) huffsize/huffcode loop and max_code/val_offset construction is reproduced almost verbatim in CUDA host jpeg.rs:50-110. That core (~40-50 LOC) is mergeable between the two std Rust backends. However the finding is overstated: (1) CPU from_raw additionally builds a FAST_ENTRIES fast/fast_dc/fast_ac packing table (huffman.rs:123-182, ~70 lines) that the CUDA host build does NOT have; (2) the DECODE paths are NOT the same implementation — CPU decode uses the fast-table lookup while CUDA main.rs and Metal use a plain max_code>=0 && code<=max_code scan, so 'decode ... duplicated' conflates distinct implementations. Only the build core is truly shareable, so est 150 is inflated ~3x.

title: YCbCr->RGB fixed-point decode conversion triplicated with identical constants
description: The 16-bit fixed-point YCbCr->RGB conversion (R=Y+1.402*Cr, etc.) is written three times with byte-identical magic constants 91881, 22554, 46802, 116130 and rounding 1<<15.
evidence: ycbcr.rs:12-15 FIX_1_40200=91_881/FIX_0_34414=22_554/FIX_0_71414=46_802/FIX_1_77200=116_130; CUDA main.rs:742-744 '91881*cr_centered+(1<<15))>>16 ... 22554*cb+46802*cr ... 116130*cb'; Metal shaders.metal:2465-2467 same three expressions with the same literals.
reduction: Define the four coefficients + ROUND once in the shared tables crate; CPU uses them directly, and build.rs emits them into the .metal/SIMT constant blocks. Conversion body is tiny but the literals are a correctness-drift hazard.
verify_note: Verified identical constants and arithmetic: CPU ycbcr.rs:12-16/56-58 (FIX_1_40200=91881, FIX_0_34414=22554, FIX_0_71414=46802, FIX_1_77200=116130, ROUND=1<<15), CUDA main.rs:742-744 (91881/22554/46802/116130, (1<<15)>>16), Metal shaders.metal:2465-2467 identical. Real correctness-drift hazard. Bodies differ only in I/O shape (CPU returns tuple, CUDA writes via &mut refs, Metal writes to out array) so the constants+ROUND are cleanly shareable and the CPU/CUDA function bodies are nearly identical. est slightly high: each copy is ~8 lines, sharing removes ~2 copies plus Metal codegen, ~20 LOC.

title: RGB->YCbCr forward encode conversion triplicated with identical constants
description: The forward RGB->YCbCr integer transform uses identical BT.601 fixed-point constants (19595, 38470, 7471 for Y; -11059, -21709, 32768, +8421376 for Cb; 32768, -27439, -5329 for Cr) in all three encoders.
reduction: Centralize the nine coefficients + bias in the shared tables crate; CPU consumes directly, build.rs codegens the Metal/SIMT constants. Optionally share the whole scalar function between CPU and CUDA-host Rust.
verify_note: Verified identical nine coefficients + biases: CPU encoder.rs:419-421 (19595/38470/7471/32768; -11059/-21709/32768/8421376; 32768/-27439/-5329/8421376), CUDA encode main.rs:244-248 identical, Metal shaders.metal:373-377 identical. Genuine constant duplication and drift hazard. Note the bodies are structurally different: CPU rgb_to_ycbcr is a single function computing all three; CUDA/Metal use rgb_to_ycbcr_component with a component-index branch, so only the constants (not the full function) share cleanly across all three. est modestly high given the small function size.

title: JPEG zigzag scan table duplicated in four places
description: The T.81 A.3.6 64-entry zigzag scan-order table is hand-written four separate times (decode CPU, decode CUDA, encode CUDA, Metal), each an identical [0,1,8,16,9,2,3,10,...,62,63] sequence.
evidence: Verified all four arrays are the same 64 values; entropy/mod.rs:14, decode main.rs:97 J2K_JPEG_ZIGZAG, encode main.rs:19 ZIGZAG, shaders.metal:299 ZIGZAG constant. A single transposition typo in one copy would silently corrupt only that backend.
reduction: Single const [u8;64] in the shared tables crate; CUDA kernels import it (Rust), Metal constant emitted by build.rs from the same array.
verify_note: Verified all four arrays are byte-identical 64-value tables: entropy/mod.rs ZIGZAG, CUDA decode main.rs:97-101 J2K_JPEG_ZIGZAG, CUDA encode main.rs:19-23 ZIGZAG, Metal shaders.metal:299-308 ZIGZAG. Three are Rust (trivially shareable via a single const in a core crate) and Metal's can be codegen'd. A single typo in one copy would corrupt only that backend, so the defect is real and the reduction is safe. est slightly high: the three Rust copies are ~6 compact lines each, so consolidation removes ~2 copies (~12 LOC) plus Metal codegen.

title: Fancy chroma upsampling (h2v2/h2v1 triangle filter) triplicated
description: libjpeg-turbo's fancy 4:2:0/4:2:2 upsampling (3*near+far weighting with edge handling) is implemented three times with the same (3,1) triangle-filter arithmetic.
evidence: CPU upsample.rs:61,66 '(3*curr+prev+2)/4' / '(3*curr+next+2)/4'; CUDA main.rs:690,703,709 'this_sum=3*curr+near; last_sum=3*last_curr+last_near; next_sum=3*next_curr+next_near'; Metal shaders.metal:2107,2118,2121 '3u*curr_row[sample]+near_row[sample]' with identical neighbor logic.
reduction: Share the scalar upsample math between CPU and CUDA-host Rust via the core crate; document the Metal port as derived. Consolidating removes an entire duplicated edge-case implementation (a frequent source of off-by-one bugs).
verify_note: The underlying libjpeg fancy (3,1)/rounding filter is the same algorithm in all three (CPU upsample.rs emit rows using (3*curr+near+round)>>2 pattern; CUDA main.rs:690-710 and Metal shaders.metal:2107-2122 use 3*curr+near sums with +8/+7 rounding), so there IS algorithmic duplication. But mergeability is doubtful: the CPU implementation is row-oriented (upsample_h2v2_fancy_rows/emit_h2v2_row operating on full prev/curr/next rows), whereas the CUDA/Metal versions are per-output-sample operating on an 8x8 block with (curr_y, bottom) edge logic — a fundamentally different data granularity, not the line-for-line copy the evidence implies. Metal further has 4+ address-space variants (h2v2_sample, _thread, _thread_local, _device_local, plus h2v1_*) that cannot be collapsed. Consolidating CPU<->CUDA would require reconciling row-vs-block models, so a clean merge is not clearly safe and est 70 is inflated.

title: Forward DCT (naive cosine FDCT) same-shape across all three encoders
location: j2k-jpeg/src/encoder.rs:709 (fdct_quantize, [[f64;8];8] cosine table); j2k-cuda-runtime/src/cuda_oxide_jpeg_encode/simt/src/main.rs:329 (jpeg_encode_fdct_quantize, COS_TABLE [[f32;8];8]); j2k-jpeg-metal/src/shaders.metal:456-481 (jpeg_encode_fdct_quantize, inline cos)
description: The forward DCT+quantize step uses the same naive separable cosine formula (0.25 * cu * cv * sum, cu/cv = 1/sqrt(2) for index 0) in all three encoders; they differ only in whether the cosine table is precomputed (CPU f64, CUDA f32 COS_TABLE) or recomputed inline (Metal).
evidence: Metal shaders.metal:474-477 'cu=(u==0)?inv_sqrt_2:1; transformed=0.25*cu*cv*sum'; CPU encoder.rs:709 fdct_quantize takes cosine:&[[f64;8];8] with the same 0.25 scaling; CUDA encode main.rs:329 uses COS_TABLE with the same structure. Same algorithm, same-shape-different-detail (table vs inline, precision).
reduction: Agree one representation (precomputed cosine table) generated from a single source; share the scalar FDCT between CPU and CUDA-host Rust. Lower priority than IDCT because encode is less hot and results are quantized, but still worth unifying the cosine table source.
verify_note: The 'same shape' claim is accurate: all three are the naive O(n^4) cosine FDCT with 0.25*cu*cv scaling (CPU encoder.rs:709-736, CUDA encode main.rs:329+ with COS_TABLE, Metal shaders.metal:456-481 inline cos). However the proposed reduction (share the scalar FDCT between CPU and CUDA-host Rust) is not sound as stated: CPU computes in f64 (cosine:&[[f64;8];8], FRAC_1_SQRT_2) while CUDA uses f32 (COS_TABLE:[[f32;8];8], INV_SQRT_2=0.70710677) and Metal f32 inline. Forcing a shared implementation would either change CPU numeric output (f64->f32) or force slow f64 on GPU, i.e. a behavior/perf change across backends. The finding itself concedes precision differs and is 'lower priority.' Only marginal wins (generating a cosine-table source that emits both f64 and f32) remain, so est 40 is inflated.

title: No shared CUDA runtime-support crate: surface wrap/upload + error mapping duplicated
description: j2k-cuda and j2k-jpeg-cuda each carry their own runtime.rs with the same functions: validate_surface_request (delegates to j2k_core::validate_cuda_surface_backend_request, identical), cuda_error (maps CudaError -> Error::CudaUnavailable/CudaRuntime, byte-for-byte identical), wrap_surface (host-path branch building a Cpu Surface with j2k_profile::emit_gpu_route_surface_profile is identical apart from the ("j2k"|"jpeg","cuda") route label), and wrap_cuda_surface / wrap_cpu_staged_cuda_surface (context.copy_with_kernel(bytes) -> into_parts() -> profile emit -> Surface build; same control flow, differ only in which stats fields are copied). The CUDA side has no analog of the j2k-metal-support crate, so this whole layer is copied. A third divergent copy of the same CudaError->Error mapping also lives in j2k-jpeg-cuda/src/owned_decode.rs:384-405.
evidence: cuda_error bodies at j2k-cuda/runtime.rs:135-143 and j2k-jpeg-cuda/runtime.rs:124-132 are identical (`if error.is_unavailable() { Error::CudaUnavailable } else { Error::CudaRuntime { message: error.to_string() } }`). validate_surface_request identical in both. wrap_surface host branch (emit_gpu_route_surface_profile + Surface{ backend: Cpu, ... Storage::Host }) identical modulo route label tuple. owned_decode.rs has cuda_owned_decode_error/cuda_chunked_entropy_diagnostic_error repeating the same match on CudaError::Unavailable/InvalidArgument.
reduction: Create a j2k-cuda-support crate (mirror of j2k-metal-support) exposing cuda_error mapping, validate_surface_request, and a generic wrap_host_surface/wrap_cuda_surface parameterized over the route label and a stats-builder closure. Have both adapter crates call it; collapse the three owned/diagnostic error mappers into one classifier with a reason parameter.
verify_note: Verified byte-identical duplication: cuda_error (j2k-cuda/runtime.rs:135-143 vs j2k-jpeg-cuda/runtime.rs:124-132) and validate_surface_request (both delegate to j2k_core::validate_cuda_surface_backend_request with identical map_err) are truly identical. The owned_decode.rs mappers (j2k-jpeg-cuda/owned_decode.rs:384-393 vs 397-406) differ only in the `reason` string and are collapsible to one classifier. A shared crate mirroring the existing j2k-metal-support is a sound, safe refactor. BUT the 110 LOC estimate is heavily inflated: the wrap_cuda_surface bodies diverge substantially (different profile fields, incompatible CudaSurfaceStats layouts, jpeg uploads on Cuda branch while j2k rejects it) so they are NOT cleanly mergeable; only cuda_error (~9), validate_surface_request (~4), the host-branch scaffold (~15), and the owned-decode collapse (~10) are realistically dedupable net. Also the host branch is 'identical modulo route label' only after ignoring j2k's extra `residency` field.

title: MetalBackendSession + lazy runtime init + runtime_initialization_error duplicated
description: Both Metal adapters define their own MetalBackendSession { device, runtime: Arc<OnceLock<Result<MetalRuntime, MetalSupportError>>>, ... } with identical new(device), system_default() (system_default_device().map(Self::new).map_err(runtime_initialization_error)), device(), AcceleratorSession impl (backend_kind = Metal), Debug impl, non-macOS stub, and a runtime() lazy get_or_init. runtime_initialization_error (MetalSupportError -> Error: is_unavailable -> MetalUnavailable else MetalRuntime{message}) is byte-for-byte identical in both compute.rs files. j2k-metal-support already provides MetalDeviceSession but neither adapter uses it because it lacks the cached-runtime slot.
evidence: runtime_initialization_error bodies identical (compute.rs:600-606 vs 400-406). MetalBackendSession::new/system_default/device identical (lib.rs:565-585 vs 692-710). Both build runtime via Arc<OnceLock<...>> get_or_init. j2k-metal only differs by extra plan-cache fields.
reduction: Extend j2k-metal-support::MetalDeviceSession to hold a generic Arc<OnceLock<Result<R, MetalSupportError>>> runtime slot with a get_or_init(builder) method, and move runtime_initialization_error into j2k-metal-support. Adapters keep only their codec-specific MetalRuntime builder + extra caches.
verify_note: Verified: runtime_initialization_error is byte-identical (j2k-metal/compute.rs:599-607 vs j2k-jpeg-metal/compute.rs:399-407). MetalBackendSession::system_default and device() are byte-identical across both lib.rs files, and both use Arc<OnceLock<Result<R, MetalSupportError>>> get_or_init for the runtime slot; j2k only adds plan-cache fields as the finding itself notes. The support crate already exposes a MetalDeviceSession with new/system_default/device that the adapters redundantly re-implement, so extending it with a generic runtime slot + moving runtime_initialization_error there is safe and sound. The 70 LOC estimate is somewhat inflated: realistic net is runtime_initialization_error (~9), system_default (~5), device (~3), AcceleratorSession (~5), Debug/new/runtime get_or_init partials — roughly 40-45 net across both crates after accounting for the generic helper.

title: CUDA adapter Error enum + CodecError impl duplicated
description: Both crates define an Error enum with the same GPU-specific variants — UnsupportedBackend { request }, UnsupportedCudaRequest { reason: &'static str }, CudaUnavailable, CudaRuntime { message } (feature-gated), plus Buffer(#[from] BufferError) and a Decode from-variant — and a CodecError impl whose is_truncated/is_not_implemented/is_unsupported/is_buffer_error bodies are identical apart from the extra JpegEncodeError arms in the jpeg crate. The CUDA-specific tail (UnsupportedBackend/UnsupportedCudaRequest/CudaUnavailable/CudaRuntime + their is_unsupported classification) is pure copy-paste.
evidence: error.rs:16-37 (j2k-cuda) vs error.rs:18-39 (j2k-jpeg-cuda) declare the same four CUDA variants with identical #[error(...)] strings. is_unsupported matches on the same UnsupportedBackend|UnsupportedCudaRequest|CudaUnavailable set in both.
reduction: Define the shared CUDA error tail as a small enum (e.g. CudaAdapterFault) or a declarative macro in j2k-cuda-support that expands the four variants + their CodecError classification, letting each crate wrap it plus its own Decode/Encode variants.
verify_note: Real duplication: UnsupportedCudaRequest, CudaUnavailable, and CudaRuntime variants are byte-identical (with identical #[error] strings) across j2k-cuda/error.rs and j2k-jpeg-cuda/error.rs, and is_unsupported matches the same UnsupportedBackend|UnsupportedCudaRequest|CudaUnavailable set. Sharing via a declarative macro/variant-tail in a support crate is safe. HOWEVER the evidence overstates: the fourth variant, UnsupportedBackend, is NOT identical — the #[error] string differs ('not supported by j2k-cuda' vs 'j2k-jpeg-cuda') and must be parameterized. CodecError impls also diverge (jpeg adds Encode arms to is_unsupported/is_buffer_error). Across only two crates, macro-based enum-variant sharing yields modest net savings once the macro definition is added; the 55 LOC estimate is heavily inflated.

title: Metal adapter Error enum + CodecError impl duplicated
description: j2k-metal and j2k-jpeg-metal define Error enums sharing the same Metal variant set: UnsupportedBackend { request }, UnsupportedMetalRequest { reason: &'static str }, MetalUnavailable, MetalRuntime { message }, MetalKernel { message }, MetalStatePoisoned { state } plus Buffer(#[from]) and a Decode from-variant, each with identical #[error(...)] strings. The CodecError impl (is_truncated/is_not_implemented/is_unsupported/is_buffer_error) is essentially identical, with jpeg adding Encode arms.
evidence: lib.rs:210-231 (j2k-metal) vs lib.rs:76-107 (j2k-jpeg-metal) declare byte-identical MetalUnavailable/MetalRuntime/MetalKernel/MetalStatePoisoned/UnsupportedMetalRequest variants. is_unsupported matches the same UnsupportedBackend|UnsupportedMetalRequest|MetalUnavailable set.
reduction: Move the shared Metal error tail + CodecError classification into j2k-metal-support (as a nested error type or a macro), leaving each adapter to compose it with its codec Decode/Encode variants.
verify_note: Real duplication: MetalUnavailable, MetalRuntime, MetalKernel, and MetalStatePoisoned variants are byte-identical between j2k-metal/lib.rs:211-231 and j2k-jpeg-metal/lib.rs:87-107, and the CodecError impls' is_truncated/is_not_implemented/is_buffer_error are byte-identical while is_unsupported covers the same UnsupportedBackend|UnsupportedMetalRequest|MetalUnavailable set. Moving the shared tail + classification into j2k-metal-support via a macro is safe. Evidence overstates 'byte-identical ... UnsupportedMetalRequest': its #[error] string differs ('unsupported J2K Metal request' vs 'unsupported JPEG Metal request') and UnsupportedBackend also differs, so both need parameterization. For only two crates net savings are modest; 55 LOC is inflated, though slightly higher potential than the CUDA case since the Metal CodecError bodies are fully identical.

title: CudaSession context/pool lazy-init + AcceleratorSession scaffolding duplicated
description: Both CudaSession types repeat: a submissions: u64 counter with submissions() accessor, DeviceSubmitSession::record_submit (saturating_add), AcceleratorSession (backend_kind = Cuda, execution_stats { submissions, ..default }), is_runtime_initialized (context.is_some()), the cuda_context() lazy initializer (`if self.context.is_none() { self.context = Some(CudaContext::system_default().map_err(cuda_error)?) }; self.context.clone().ok_or(CudaUnavailable)` — identical), and a lazy CudaBufferPool getter (`if let Some(pool) = &self.x { return Ok(pool.clone()) } let pool = self.cuda_context()?.buffer_pool(); self.x = Some(pool.clone()); Ok(pool)` — repeated once in jpeg, three times in j2k for the different pools). Debug impls follow the same field-listing pattern.
evidence: cuda_context() bodies identical (session.rs:47-52 vs 145-150). decode_buffer_pool/decode_batch_buffer_pool/owned_output_pool are the same memoize-on-context pattern (session.rs:79-98 vs 153-160). record_submit + AcceleratorSession impls identical in both.
reduction: Provide a CudaDeviceSession helper in j2k-cuda-support owning the context + a lazy_pool(&mut self, selector) memoizer and the DeviceSubmitSession/AcceleratorSession impls; each adapter session embeds it and adds only codec-specific caches (HTJ2K tables, owned packet cache).
verify_note: Verified byte-identical: cuda_context() (j2k-cuda/session.rs:47-52 vs j2k-jpeg-cuda/session.rs:145-150), DeviceSubmitSession::record_submit (saturating_add), and the AcceleratorSession impl (backend_kind->Cuda, execution_stats{submissions}). The pool memoizers follow the same memoize-on-context pattern differing only in the pool selector (buffer_pool vs best_fit_buffer_pool vs owned_output_pool). A CudaDeviceSession helper in a support crate with a generic lazy_pool(selector) plus the shared trait impls is a sound refactor; each adapter would keep only its codec-specific caches (HTJ2K tables / owned packet cache). The 55 LOC estimate is somewhat inflated; realistic net is ~35-40 (cuda_context ~6, submit/accelerator ~15, pool memoize generalization).

title: Metal DeviceSurface impl + Surface/Storage wrappers duplicated
description: The DeviceSurface impl for the Metal Surface type is near-identical in both adapters: residency() maps the local SurfaceResidency enum (Host/MetalResidentDecode/CpuStagedMetalUpload) to j2k_core::SurfaceResidency with the same three arms, dimensions()/pixel_format() are trivial, byte_len() = pitch_bytes * height, and memory_range() builds a DeviceMemoryRange from buffer.as_ptr()/offset/byte_len with the same StorageMode-null handling. The Surface struct fields (backend, residency, dimensions, fmt, pitch_bytes, offset, storage) and Storage { Host(Vec<u8>), Metal(...) } are the same shape (jpeg carries buffer+offset in the Metal variant, j2k a plain Buffer + separate byte_offset field).
evidence: residency() match arms identical (lib.rs:511-521 vs 265-275). byte_len() = self.pitch_bytes * self.dimensions.1 in both. memory_range() constructs DeviceMemoryRange::new(BackendKind::Metal, ptr, offset, byte_len()) identically.
reduction: Introduce a generic Metal Surface wrapper (or shared DeviceSurface blanket helper) in j2k-metal-support parameterized over the Storage variant, sharing residency-mapping and memory_range logic.
verify_note: Verified: DeviceSurface::residency match arms are byte-identical (j2k-metal/lib.rs:512-522 vs j2k-jpeg-metal/lib.rs:265-275), as are byte_len, backend_kind, dimensions, and pixel_format. memory_range is structurally identical but not byte-identical — the offset source differs (j2k uses a Surface.byte_offset field with Storage::Metal(buffer); jpeg uses Storage::Metal{buffer,offset}), so consolidation needs a small trait/accessor to abstract that. residency mapping can be a shared free function; the rest via a generic wrapper. 5 of 6 trait methods are duplicated so the finding is real, but the 45 LOC estimate is a bit high given the memory_range/Storage divergence requires added abstraction; realistic net ~30.

title: Entire JP2 box-parsing stack reimplemented in facade and native engine
location: j2k/src/parse/boxes.rs (whole file, 756 lines) vs j2k-native/src/jp2/mod.rs + jp2/box.rs + jp2/cdef.rs + jp2/cmap.rs + jp2/colr.rs + jp2/pclr.rs; parallel types in j2k/src/metadata.rs:112-210
description: Both crates independently parse the JP2 container from scratch: box header decode (LBox/TBox/XLBox with the 0/1/len cases), ftyp brand classification for jp2/jph, ihdr, bpcc, colr, pclr, cmap, cdef, the component-descriptor byte decode, and the ihdr-vs-codestream + component-precision validation. j2k-native builds internal types (ImageHeaderBox, ComponentDescriptor, ColorSpace/EnumeratedColorspace, ChannelType/ChannelAssociation, ComponentMappingType, PaletteColumn) while j2k builds the near-identical public family (J2kComponentInfo, J2kColorSpec, J2kChannelType/J2kChannelAssociation, J2kComponentMappingType, J2kPaletteColumn). The facade already depends on j2k-native, so this is a duplicated implementation, not an isolation boundary.
evidence: read_box_header (boxes.rs:679-756) vs box::read (jp2/box.rs:69-102): same LBox 0/1/else logic, same xlbox-16 / len-8 subtraction. classify_file_type (boxes.rs:233-244) is byte-for-byte the same as classify_file_type (jp2/mod.rs:188-202): `payload[..4]==b"jph "` then chunks_exact(4) over payload[8..]. parse_ihdr (boxes.rs:322-346) == parse_image_header (jp2/mod.rs:221-239): same [11]!=7 check, same height/width/components field order. parse_component_descriptor (boxes.rs:510-525) == jp2/mod.rs:250-259: `(descriptor & 0x7f)+1`, `>38` reject, `&0x80` signed. parse_pclr (boxes.rs:527-588) == pclr.rs parse (bit_depth (&0x7f)+1, div_ceil(8).max(1), big-endian raw accumulate). parse_cmap (boxes.rs:590-615) == cmap.rs (0=Direct,1=Palette{column}). parse_cdef (boxes.rs:617-670) == cdef.rs (0=Colour,1=Opacity,2=Premul,MAX=Unspecified; assoc 0=WholeImage,MAX=Unspecified,else Color). parse_colr (boxes.rs:467-480) mirrors colr.rs method 1/2 handling. resolved_image_component_metadata (boxes.rs:417-452) == resolved_image_component_descriptors (jp2/mod.rs:313-363).
reduction: Extract a single #![no_std] JP2 box-parsing module (new j2k-jp2-parse crate, or add to j2k-native and re-export) that owns box-walking + the ihdr/bpcc/colr/pclr/cmap/cdef primitive decoders and the box-type constants, returning neutral data (place the shared enums in j2k-types). Have j2k-native and the j2k facade both consume it and map to their local error/metadata types via thin From impls instead of re-parsing bytes.
verify_note: The parallel reimplementation is real and verified line-by-line: classify_file_type (boxes.rs:233-244 vs jp2/mod.rs:188-202) is functionally identical (payload[..4]==b"jph " then chunks_exact(4) over payload[8..]); parse_ihdr (boxes.rs:322-346) == parse_image_header (mod.rs:221-239) with the same data[11]!=7 check and height/width/components ordering; parse_component_descriptor is identical ((d&0x7f)+1, >38 reject, &0x80 signed) in both; read_box_header (boxes.rs:679-756) and box::read (box.rs:69-102) implement the same LBox 0/1/else scheme with the xlbox-16 subtraction; pclr/cmap/cdef decoders match. This is genuine duplication. However the merge is harder than presented: it is cross-crate with two different error models (J2kError{offset,what} with rich diagnostics vs flat FormatError), different data types (J2kComponentInfo carries x_rsiz/y_rsiz, ComponentDescriptor does not), different substrate (facade uses offset slicing, native uses BitReader), and native has behaviors the facade lacks (strict/non-strict fail-soft in cdef/pclr, settings.target_resolution=None on palette) while the facade enforces box ordering the native does not. A neutral shared parser would strip the facade's per-offset error messages unless parameterized, and requires new neutral types in j2k-types. Realistic net savings after glue on both sides and new shared types is well below 300.

title: SIZ/COD segment parse + validation and marker constants duplicated inside j2k-native
description: j2k-native carries a lightweight header inspector (inspect.rs) and the full decode-path parser (j2c/codestream.rs) that parse the same SIZ and COD marker segments and apply the same structural validation, plus inspect.rs declares its own private copy of the codestream marker-code constants that already exist in the j2c::markers module.
evidence: inspect.rs:12-18 defines MARKER_SOC=0x4F/CAP=0x50/SIZ=0x51/COD=0x52/SOT=0x90/SOD=0x93/EOC=0xD9, identical values to j2c/codestream.rs markers module (SOC 0x4F, CAP 0x50, SIZ 0x51, COD 0x52, SOT 0x90, SOD 0x93, EOC 0xD9 at lines 1248-1259). SIZ validation is duplicated: inspect.rs parse_siz (296-410) checks tile_width/height!=0, tile origin < image size, tile origin > image origin, tile extent overlaps image, component sampling !=0, dims<=MAX_J2K_IMAGE_DIMENSION, tile_count<=MAX_J2K_TILE_COUNT; size_marker (codestream.rs:690-751) repeats the same seven checks against MAX_J2K_IMAGE_DIMENSION / MAX_J2K_TILE_COUNT. Component precision decode `(ssiz & 0x7F)+1` / `ssiz & 0x80` appears in both (inspect.rs:385-386 and the size_marker component loop). COD decode of resolution levels / MCT / reversible / HT (inspect.rs parse_cod:412-424) overlaps cod_marker (codestream.rs:1043-1069).
reduction: Have inspect.rs reuse the j2c markers module for its constants, and factor the shared SIZ dimension/tile/precision validation into one helper (e.g. validate_siz_geometry) called by both size_marker and the inspector so the MAX_* bounds and overflow checks live in one place; inspect can keep its own light struct but stop re-implementing the checks.
verify_note: Verified: inspect.rs:12-18 defines MARKER_SOC=0x4F/CAP=0x50/SIZ=0x51/COD=0x52/SOT=0x90/SOD=0x93/EOC=0xD9, identical values to the markers module (codestream.rs:1248-1259). SIZ geometry validation is genuinely duplicated: both check tile_width/height!=0, tile origin < image size, tile origin <= image-area origin, first-tile overlap with checked_add overflow guards, csiz==0 / csiz>MAX_J2K_SPEC_COMPONENTS, x_origin<x_size, and tile_count<=MAX_J2K_TILE_COUNT (inspect parse_siz:328-380 vs size_marker:693-751 + size_marker_inner:790-800). Component precision decode (ssiz&0x7F)+1 / &0x80 appears in both. Importing the markers module for the 7 constants and factoring a validate_siz_geometry helper is safe. BUT not all checks are equivalent: inspect caps dimensions on (x_size - x_origin) whereas size_marker uses image_width()/height() with shrink-factor div_ceil; inspect applies no component-precision upper bound while codestream rejects >MAX_PART1_COMPONENT_PRECISION; and the two use distinct error taxonomies (InvalidSiz{what} vs ValidationError/TileError/MarkerError). So only the constants and the tile-geometry subset merge cleanly; 120 LOC overstates the safely-shareable portion.

title: Two near-identical JPEG marker-segment walkers in j2k-jpeg
description: The internal decode path (MarkerWalker) and the public segment API (JpegSegmentIter) each implement the same JPEG marker-stream state machine: read SOI (FFD8), skip fill 0xFF bytes, classify standalone markers, and read a length-prefixed payload with length<2 and truncation checks. Only the emitted struct (Marker vs JpegSegment) and minor standalone-set details differ.
evidence: read_soi is duplicated almost verbatim: markers.rs:64-81 and segment.rs:600-621 both require len>=2, check bytes[0]!=0xFF || bytes[1]!=0xD8 -> UnexpectedMarker{Soi}, set pos=2. The marker read loop matches: markers.rs:104-112 and segment.rs:630-638 both reject non-0xFF then `while pos<len && bytes[pos]==0xFF { pos+=1 }`. Length-prefixed handling is the same: markers.rs:149-165 and segment.rs:672-691 both read u16 BE length, reject length<2 (InvalidSegmentLength), compute payload_end with overflow/truncation guard. Both crates also share next_marker_after_entropy (segment.rs imports it from parse::markers, line 194) confirming the walkers are meant to be one component.
reduction: Make segment.rs's JpegSegmentIter delegate to MarkerWalker (or extract a single shared low-level walker producing raw {code, marker_offset, payload_offset, payload}), and have the public JpegSegment be a thin newtype/adapter over it, deleting the duplicated read_soi/read_segment body.
verify_note: Strongest of the three and same-crate. read_soi is duplicated near-verbatim: markers.rs:63-80 and segment.rs:600-621 both require len>=2 -> Truncated, check bytes[0]!=0xFF||bytes[1]!=0xD8 -> UnexpectedMarker{Soi}, set pos=2. The marker-prefix loop matches: both reject non-0xFF -> InvalidMarker then while pos<len && bytes[pos]==0xFF { pos+=1 } with the same Truncated guards (markers.rs:104-118, segment.rs:630-644). Length-prefix handling matches: u16 BE length, length<2 -> InvalidSegmentLength, payload-end bounds -> Truncated (markers.rs:143-165, segment.rs:666-693). segment.rs already imports next_marker_after_entropy from parse::markers (line 584), confirming these are meant to be one low-level walker. The proposed extraction (shared raw walker emitting {code, marker_offset, payload_offset, payload}) is sound because the genuine behavioral differences stay in per-consumer classification: markers treats 0x00 as a standalone empty marker and returns None at EOI (0xD9), while segment treats 0x00 as InvalidMarker, treats 0xD9 as a finishing standalone marker, and sets scan_entropy on 0xDA; segment also adds a checked_add overflow guard markers lacks. Those must be preserved via an adapter, so 90 slightly overstates net savings after glue.

title: Four GPU-adapter Error enums + CodecError impls are near-identical
description: Each of the four device-adapter crates defines a top-level `Error` enum with the same skeleton: `Decode(#[from] <cpu error>)`, `Buffer(#[from] BufferError)`, `UnsupportedBackend { request: BackendRequest }`, an adapter-specific `UnsupportedXRequest { reason: &'static str }`, an `XUnavailable` variant, and an `XRuntime { message: String }` variant (the Metal pair additionally repeats `MetalKernel`/`MetalStatePoisoned` verbatim, the JPEG pair adds `Encode(#[from] JpegEncodeError)`). Each then hand-writes an identical `impl CodecError` whose four methods delegate `is_truncated`/`is_not_implemented`/`is_buffer_error` to the inner `Decode` and OR the unsupported/unavailable variants into `is_unsupported`.
evidence: The `impl CodecError for Error` blocks in j2k-cuda/error.rs:40-62 and j2k-metal/src/lib.rs:234-256 are token-for-token the same except the enum variant list; j2k-jpeg-cuda and j2k-jpeg-metal add the same two extra Encode arms. All four `is_truncated`/`is_not_implemented` bodies are literally `matches!(self, Self::Decode(inner) if inner.is_truncated())`. All four define `UnsupportedBackend { request: BackendRequest }` with the same error string pattern `"backend request {request:?} is not supported by <crate>"`.
reduction: Add a small `adapter_error!{}` declarative macro (or a generic `AdapterError<D>` wrapper + blanket `CodecError` impl) in j2k-core that generates the shared `Decode`/`Buffer`/`UnsupportedBackend` variants and the delegating CodecError impl, letting each crate declare only its device-specific variants. At minimum extract the 4 identical CodecError impls into one macro.
verify_note: Verified in j2k-cuda/error.rs:40-62, j2k-jpeg-cuda/error.rs:42-79, j2k-metal/lib.rs:234-256, j2k-jpeg-metal/lib.rs:110-132. The is_truncated/is_not_implemented/is_buffer_error bodies are literally identical across all four (matches!(self, Self::Decode(inner) if inner.is_truncated()) etc.). is_unsupported is token-for-token identical between cuda and metal except the device variant names (UnsupportedCudaRequest/CudaUnavailable vs UnsupportedMetalRequest/MetalUnavailable); the two jpeg crates add the same Encode arms. All four declare UnsupportedBackend { request: BackendRequest } with the same 'backend request {request:?} is not supported by <crate>' string. A declarative macro parameterized over the unsupported-variant list can genuinely generate these. est_loc_saved 90 is inflated: the four CodecError impls total ~92 physical lines but a macro still needs 4 invocations plus its own definition; realistic net saving after also folding the 3 shared enum variants is ~50.

title: RouteDecision enum + decision_error/profile mapping duplicated across Metal adapters
description: Both Metal adapter crates define a private `RouteDecision` enum with the same variants (`CpuHost`, `MetalKernel`, `RejectExplicitMetal { reason }`, `RejectUnsupportedBackend { request }`, `MetalUnavailable`), a matching `ExplicitMetalRejection` with `error_reason`/`profile_reason` accessors, a `decide_route` that maps BackendRequest to a decision (Cpu/Auto->CpuHost, Metal->kernel-or-reject, Cuda->RejectUnsupportedBackend), a `decision_error` that maps the decision to the crate `Error`, and a route->MetalRouteProfileLabels mapping using j2k-metal-support helpers.
evidence: j2k-metal routing.rs `decision_error` (106-121) and j2k-jpeg-metal routing.rs `decision_error` (148-159) produce the same `Error::UnsupportedMetalRequest`/`Error::UnsupportedBackend`/`Error::MetalUnavailable`/None arms. Both `RouteDecision` enums and both profile-label match arms (j2k-metal 127-139 vs j2k-jpeg-metal 30-42) call the identical cpu_host_route/metal_kernel_route/reject_*_route helpers. The BackendRequest::Cuda arm is identical in both `decide_route`s.
reduction: Move the shared `RouteDecision` shape, its `decision_error` mapping (parameterized over the crate Error via a tiny trait or closure), and the profile-label mapping into j2k-metal-support next to MetalRouteProfileLabels; keep only the per-crate capability/format predicate. The `ExplicitMetalRejection` detail differs, so make the reason payload a `&'static str`.
verify_note: Both RouteDecision enums (j2k-metal/routing.rs:16-29, j2k-jpeg-metal/routing.rs:15-28) have the identical shape (CpuHost, MetalKernel, RejectExplicitMetal{reason}, RejectUnsupportedBackend{request}, MetalUnavailable). decision_error is structurally identical (RejectExplicitMetal->UnsupportedMetalRequest{reason}, RejectUnsupportedBackend->UnsupportedBackend{request}, MetalUnavailable->MetalUnavailable, CpuHost/MetalKernel->None) at metal:106-121 vs jpeg-metal:148-159, and the profile-label mapping calls the same j2k-metal-support helpers (cpu_host_route/metal_kernel_route/reject_*_route/metal_unavailable_route). The ExplicitMetalRejection payload genuinely differs (UnsupportedFormat{fmt} vs MissingFastPacket/UnsupportedOutputFormat), which the finding correctly proposes to handle by carrying &'static str. Minor wrinkle: metal uses cfg gating while jpeg-metal uses allow(dead_code), so the merge is slightly less clean than described. Merge is feasible via a trait over the crate Error. est 60 is high: the duplicated shared code (enum ~13 + decision_error ~14 + profile map ~11 per crate) is ~38 lines of one copy; net saving after generic/trait scaffolding is ~40.

title: CudaTranscodeError and MetalTranscodeError are same-shape twins
description: The two transcode accelerator crates define parallel error enums with the same variant shape (`<Device>Unavailable`, `UnsupportedJob(&'static str)`, `Kernel(&'static str)`, plus Metal's extra `Runtime(&'static str)`), the same manual `Display` (unavailable const string, all `&str` arms just write the reason), the same manual `impl std::error::Error {}`, and the same `From<Self> for TranscodeStageError` mapping (Unavailable->DeviceUnavailable, UnsupportedJob->Unsupported, Kernel/Runtime->Backend(to_string)).
evidence: j2k-transcode-cuda `From<CudaTranscodeError> for TranscodeStageError` (lib.rs:75-83) and j2k-transcode-metal `From<MetalTranscodeError> for TranscodeStageError` (lib.rs:74-83) are structurally identical mappings into the same j2k-transcode `TranscodeStageError`. Both Display impls (cuda 65-73, metal 63-72) collapse the reason-bearing arms to `f.write_str(reason)`. Both derive the same `Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq` and add `is_recoverable`/recoverable-set logic on `Unavailable | UnsupportedJob`.
reduction: Define one generic accelerator error (e.g. `TranscodeAcceleratorError` in j2k-transcode) with variants Unavailable/Unsupported(&str)/Runtime(&str)/Kernel(&str), a single Display, a single `From` into TranscodeStageError, and a single `is_recoverable`; the two GPU crates alias it or wrap it with only the device label differing.
verify_note: Confirmed the essential duplication: both Display impls collapse reason-bearing arms to f.write_str(reason) (cuda:65-72, metal:62-71), both From impls map into the same TranscodeStageError with the same DeviceUnavailable/Unsupported/Backend targets (cuda:74-82, metal:73-83), and both derive Debug,Clone,Copy,PartialEq,Eq plus a manual std::error::Error impl. However the evidence overstates 'same-shape': MetalTranscodeError has an extra Runtime(&str) variant (4 variants vs cuda's 3), and is_recoverable exists ONLY in j2k-transcode-cuda (lib.rs:56-63, used at 297) -- j2k-transcode-metal has no is_recoverable, so 'Both... add is_recoverable' is false. The Display consts also differ (CUDA_UNAVAILABLE vs METAL_UNAVAILABLE). These are pub types used at call sites (e.g. MetalTranscodeError::Runtime in metal.rs), so a generic TranscodeAcceleratorError merge is feasible but touches public API and requires rewriting variant references. Duplication is real but modest (~26 lines of one copy); est 45 is inflated to ~25.

title: Parallel accelerator wrapper state machines (Cuda vs Metal DctToWaveletStageAccelerator)
description: Both crates implement the same j2k-transcode `DctToWaveletStageAccelerator` trait by wrapping it in an almost identical harness: a two-variant dispatch-mode enum (Explicit/Auto), a large counter/threshold struct (min_auto_samples, min_auto_*_batch_jobs/samples, reversible_dwt53_attempts/dispatches, reversible_dwt53_batch_attempts/dispatches, dwt53_attempts/dispatches, dwt97_attempts/dispatches, dwt97_batch_attempts/dispatches, htj2k97_codeblock_batch_attempts/dispatches, last_dwt97_batch_stage_timings, plus an optional session), a `for_auto`/`new_explicit`/`with_mode` constructor set, ~13 identical `*_attempts()/*_dispatches()` const getters, and per-trait-method bodies that follow the same protocol: `self.x_attempts += 1`, an Auto sample/jobs threshold gate returning `Ok(None)`, a `#[cfg(not(backend))]` block mapping Explicit->Err(DeviceUnavailable)/Auto->Ok(None), then a `#[cfg(backend)]` match on the dispatch that bumps `*_dispatches` and stores timings on success and calls recover()/inline error mapping on failure. The only real divergences are cfg keys (feature="cuda-runtime" vs target_os="macos"), CUDA's extra resident_ht_encode preencoded/compact/group methods, and Metal's session/device constructors + MAX_AUTO_DWT97_STAGED_BATCH_AXIS cap.
evidence: Struct fields line up 1:1: cuda lib.rs:97-120 vs metal lib.rs:319-343. Constructors: cuda:126-177 (with_mode) vs metal:355-412 (new_explicit/for_auto). Getters: cuda:212-281 vs metal:514-584 are the same names/bodies. Dispatch protocol repeats identically per method, e.g. dct_grid_to_reversible_dwt53 cuda:365-394 vs metal:667-702, dct_grid_to_dwt97_batch cuda:492-526 vs metal:783-835, dct_grid_to_htj2k97_codeblock_batch cuda:528-569 vs metal:837-898. Error recovery cuda:283-302 (unavailable/recover) mirrors metal's inline `Err(MetalUnavailable|UnsupportedJob(_)) if Auto => Ok(None)` arms.
reduction: Introduce a generic harness in j2k-transcode (e.g. `StageAcceleratorHarness<B: TranscodeBackendHooks>`) that owns the mode enum, counter/threshold fields, getters, sample-summing, and the attempts/gate/dispatch/dispatches/recover flow, delegating only the actual GPU call and availability to a small backend-hooks trait. Each crate then keeps a thin `B` impl (dispatch_* fns + cfg availability) plus its unique methods (CUDA resident_ht_encode paths, Metal session/device ctors).
verify_note: Real, substantial duplication. The counter fields (attempts/dispatches pairs), the mode enum (Explicit/Auto), the ~15 const getters (lib.rs cuda:212-281 vs metal:514-584 are name/body identical), the sample-sum helpers, and the per-method attempt-count -> auto-gate -> cfg-dispatch -> count-dispatch -> recover flow are genuinely parallel and could be hoisted into a generic harness + backend-hooks trait in j2k-transcode. HOWEVER the finding's 'fields line up 1:1' claim is overstated: metal carries min_auto_dwt97_samples and min_auto_reversible_samples (separate per-transform thresholds) that CUDA folds into a single min_auto_samples, CUDA carries resident_ht_encode plus ~250 lines of unique resident/i16/compact/group methods (cuda:571-824) metal lacks, metal adds a MAX_AUTO_DWT97_STAGED_BATCH_AXIS gate in its batch paths (metal:803-810,860-867) absent in CUDA, the cfg guards differ (feature="cuda-runtime" vs target_os="macos"), and error recovery is a method (recover) in CUDA vs inline match arms in metal. A harness can preserve behavior with enough hook points but the divergences make it a large, risky refactor and cap realistic dedup well below 320 lines (much of CUDA's block is non-shared unique methods). Duplication is genuine and mergeable in principle, so CONFIRMED, but est_loc_saved is inflated.

title: Near-identical backend error enum + Display + From-for-TranscodeStageError + fallback mapping
description: CudaTranscodeError and MetalTranscodeError are the same shape: an Unavailable variant, UnsupportedJob(&'static str), and Kernel(&'static str) (Metal adds a Runtime(&'static str) that maps the same as Kernel). Both have a Display impl that writes the unavailable constant or forwards the reason, and a `From<..> for TranscodeStageError` that maps Unavailable->DeviceUnavailable, UnsupportedJob->Unsupported, Kernel/Runtime->Backend(reason.to_string()). Both crates also define the same Explicit->Err / Auto->Ok(None) recovery decision for unavailable/unsupported errors.
evidence: cuda lib.rs:65-84 (Display + From) vs metal lib.rs:62-85 are structurally identical; recoverability classifier cuda `is_recoverable` (60-62) matches metal's inline `matches!(.., MetalUnavailable | UnsupportedJob(_))` guards on the dispatch arms.
reduction: Provide a shared `TranscodeBackendError`-style enum (or a blanket `From`+recoverable() helper) in j2k-transcode parameterized by an unavailable message const, so each backend only supplies its label constant instead of re-declaring the enum, Display, From and recover logic.
verify_note: The CudaTranscodeError (lib.rs:46-84) and MetalTranscodeError (lib.rs:49-85) enums with their Display, From<..> for TranscodeStageError, and recoverable classifier are structurally parallel and could share a parameterized enum/helper keyed on the unavailable-message const. But they are NOT identical: MetalTranscodeError has a fourth variant Runtime(&'static str) (mapped alongside Kernel in both Display and From) that CudaTranscodeError lacks, and the recoverability logic is a named const fn is_recoverable in CUDA vs inline matches!(MetalUnavailable | UnsupportedJob(_)) arms in metal. Both are pub types, so unifying variant sets is a mild API change but doable. Duplication is real and safely mergeable, so CONFIRMED, but the Runtime-variant divergence and the fact each enum is only ~36-38 lines means 45 saved lines is inflated.

title: Batch sample-sum / Auto job-count-and-samples threshold gate reimplemented per crate and per method
location: crates/j2k-transcode-cuda/src/lib.rs:305-337 (5 *_total_samples fns) and the per-method Auto gates; crates/j2k-transcode-metal/src/lib.rs:613-624,794-810,851-867 (inline folds + gates)
description: The 'sum of width*height over jobs' fold and the Auto-mode gate `if jobs.len() < min_jobs || total_samples < min_samples { return Ok(None) }` are written repeatedly. CUDA factors five near-identical `*_total_samples` fold functions (reversible/dwt97/codeblock/i16/group) that differ only in the job type; Metal inlines the exact same fold three times. The single-job gate `job.width*job.height < min_samples => Ok(None)` is likewise repeated in every scalar method of both crates.
evidence: cuda lib.rs:305-309/311-315/317-321/323-329/331-337 are the same fold over different job slices; metal lib.rs:613-615, 794-796, 851-853 duplicate that fold inline; single-job gates appear at cuda:371-374/438-440/466-469 and metal:674-677/717-720/753-756.
reduction: Add a `BatchGeometry`/`JobSamples` trait (job -> width,height) on the shared job types in j2k-transcode and one generic `total_samples(jobs)` + `auto_declines(mode, jobs, min_jobs, min_samples)` helper; fold both into the shared harness from finding 1 so no backend restates the arithmetic.
verify_note: The fold `jobs.iter().fold(0usize, |t, job| t.saturating_add(job.width.saturating_mul(job.height)))` is genuinely identical across cuda's reversible/dwt97/codeblock/i16 total-samples fns (lib.rs:305-329) and metal's inline copies (lib.rs:613-615,794-796,851-853) — ~7 identical copies. All four job types live in the shared j2k-transcode crate (accelerator.rs:34-137) and expose width/height, so a JobSamples trait + generic total_samples is feasible and behavior-preserving. Minor corrections: the 5th cuda fn htj2k97_i16_codeblock_batch_group_total_samples (331-337) is a nested group-level fold, not the same simple fold, and metal's batch gates additionally include a MAX_AUTO_DWT97_STAGED_BATCH_AXIS check the CUDA gate omits, so the auto_declines helper can't be perfectly uniform. Real duplication, CONFIRMED, but after accounting for trait-impl boilerplate and the non-identical group fold, 60 lines is optimistic.

title: Uniform-batch-geometry validation copy-pasted 5x within cuda.rs (and same intent in metal.rs)
description: The check that every job in a batch shares block_cols/block_rows/width/height with the first job is duplicated five times across cuda.rs dispatch functions, each with the same `jobs.iter().all(|job| job.block_cols == first.block_cols && ...)` and a near-identical UnsupportedJob error string. Metal enforces the same same-geometry contract in its staged batch path, so the concept crosses crates too.
evidence: cuda.rs:371-376 (dwt97 batch), 543-549 (codeblock batch), 604-612 (resident HT batch), 674-682 (resident HT i16 batch), 790-798 (grouped) all repeat the four-field equality over `first`; the referenced job types (DctGridToDwt97Job/DctGridToHtj2k97CodeBlockJob/DctGridI16ToHtj2k97CodeBlockJob) expose the same four fields.
reduction: Add a `fn is_uniform_geometry(jobs)` (or `first_geometry().matches(job)`) on a shared job-geometry trait in j2k-transcode and call it from every batch dispatcher, passing the crate-specific error string.
verify_note: Verified 5 byte-for-byte-equivalent copies of the four-field equality check `jobs.iter().all(|job| job.block_cols==first.block_cols && job.block_rows==first.block_rows && job.width==first.width && job.height==first.height)` at cuda.rs:371-376, 543-549, 604-612, 674-682, and 790-798 (last over group.jobs). All referenced job types (DctGridToDwt97Job, DctGridToHtj2k97CodeBlockJob, DctGridI16ToHtj2k97CodeBlockJob) are defined in the shared j2k-transcode crate and all carry the same four fields (accelerator.rs:85-137), so a shared is_uniform_geometry helper/trait is feasible and behavior-preserving (each call site keeps its own error string). CONFIRMED, but each block is only ~6 lines and a generic solution needs a geometry-accessor trait with per-type impls, so net line savings are well under the claimed 30.

title: JPEG->HTJ2K route/fallback facade exists only in Metal; CUDA will duplicate it when added
description: j2k-transcode-metal wraps the transcoder with a backend-routing facade: a BackendRequest match (Cpu/Auto/Metal/Cuda) building the right accelerator, a strict-dispatch guard, and a RouteReport carrying selected backend + structured fallback reason derived from TranscodeTimingReport counters. j2k-transcode-cuda currently exposes only the raw accelerator with no route facade, so the moment CUDA needs the same request-routing surface this ~200-line block will be re-implemented verbatim with s/Metal/Cuda/. The report/fallback derivation (selected_transform_backend, fallback_reason, ensure_strict_*) is backend-agnostic today.
evidence: metal lib.rs:176-300 implement request routing + report; grep for route_report/FallbackReason/RouteReport across j2k-transcode-cuda/src returned nothing, confirming CUDA has no equivalent yet. The logic keys off shared j2k-transcode types (BackendRequest, TranscodeTimingReport::accelerator_work_observed/pipeline_map).
reduction: Lift the request->accelerator routing, strict-dispatch guard, and RouteReport/FallbackReason construction into j2k-transcode as a generic `transcode_with_route<A: DctToWaveletStageAccelerator>(request, make_explicit, make_auto)` helper (backend enum passed in), so CUDA gets the facade for free instead of cloning it.

title: Per-subband inverse quantization delta closure duplicated across backends
description: The mapping from Htj2k97CodeBlockOptions to per-subband inverse step sizes `(1.0 / htj2k97_subband_delta(options, sub)) as f32` for the four subbands is written as a closure twice in cuda.rs and once as `dwt97_quantize_inv_delta` in metal.rs. The underlying htj2k97_subband_delta is already shared in j2k-transcode; only this thin inv-delta wrapper is copied.
evidence: cuda.rs:558 and 908 both define `|sub| (1.0/htj2k97_subband_delta(options,sub)) as f32` and build LL/HL/LH/HH params; metal.rs:2365-2370 defines the same as a named fn used to fill the four bands.
reduction: Expose a single `htj2k97_subband_inv_delta_f32(options, sub)` (and/or a `[f32;4]` builder) next to htj2k97_subband_delta in j2k-transcode and call it from both backends.
verify_note: Verified: cuda.rs:557-558 and 907-908 both define the closure `|sub: J2kSubBandType| -> f32 { (1.0 / htj2k97_subband_delta(options, sub)) as f32 }` and populate LL/HL/LH/HH params, and metal.rs:2365-2370 defines dwt97_quantize_inv_delta with the exact same body. htj2k97_subband_delta already lives in the shared j2k-transcode crate (htj2k97_codeblock_oracle.rs:129), so exposing a single htj2k97_subband_inv_delta_f32 helper (and/or a [f32;4] builder) next to it and calling from both backends is trivially safe and behavior-identical. CONFIRMED; est is roughly right but slightly high given each closure is only 1-2 lines.

title: J2K codestream marker-scan test helpers duplicated across native + GPU transcode tests
description: The same tiny codestream-parsing helpers are re-typed in many test files: `marker_offset` (identical body: windows(2).position(==[0xFF,marker]).unwrap_or_else(panic)) in plt_decode.rs, poc_decode.rs, tile_header_markers.rs; `find_marker` (same, returning Option) in native encode_coefficients.rs and in both transcode-cuda and transcode-metal jpeg_to_htj2k.rs; `cod_marker_end` byte-identical in poc_decode.rs and tile_header_markers.rs; and `assert_component_sampling` byte-identical in transcode-cuda and transcode-metal jpeg_to_htj2k.rs.
evidence: Read all sites: marker_offset bodies at plt_decode.rs:5-10, poc_decode.rs:5-10, tile_header_markers.rs:9-14 are character-identical; find_marker at encode_coefficients.rs:473-477, transcode-cuda jpeg_to_htj2k.rs:99-103, transcode-metal jpeg_to_htj2k.rs:449-453 identical; cod_marker_end at poc_decode.rs:12-16 == tile_header_markers.rs:16-20; assert_component_sampling at transcode-cuda:89-97 == transcode-metal:438-446.
reduction: Put a shared `codestream_markers` test util in j2k-transcode (both GPU transcode crates already depend on it) for find_marker/assert_component_sampling, and a `tests/common/mod.rs` inside j2k-native for marker_offset/cod_marker_end/find_marker (native intentionally has no test-support dep). Then delete the per-file copies.
verify_note: Verified all cited sites. marker_offset bodies at plt_decode.rs:5-10, poc_decode.rs:5-10, tile_header_markers.rs:9-14 are character-identical. cod_marker_end is identical between poc_decode.rs:12-16 and tile_header_markers.rs:16-20. find_marker (0xff variant) is identical across encode_coefficients.rs:473-477, transcode-cuda jpeg_to_htj2k.rs:99-103, transcode-metal jpeg_to_htj2k.rs:449-453 (metal cfg-gated). assert_component_sampling is identical between cuda:89-97 and metal:438-446. Consolidation is sound via a native tests/common/mod.rs (marker_offset/cod_marker_end/find_marker) and shared test util. Note cross-crate sharing for the GPU helpers requires pub/test-feature exposure, adding a little boilerplate. Net removable lines are lower than claimed after accounting for the retained canonical copies and new shared-module scaffolding.

title: minimal_baseline_jpeg fixture family re-implemented in j2k-jpeg inspect test despite being exported by test-support
description: inspect.rs defines local `minimal_baseline_jpeg` and `minimal_baseline_jpeg_with_restart_interval` that are byte-for-byte the same construction as j2k_test_support::minimal_baseline_jpeg / minimal_baseline_jpeg_with_restart_interval. The file even carries a stale comment 'duplicated here because integration tests cannot access pub(crate) helpers', yet the shared crate already exports these and inspect.rs already imports it as `use j2k_test_support as fixtures`.
evidence: inspect.rs:22-56 emits SOI, DQT(67), SOF0 with sampling (2<<4)|2=0x22 / (1<<4)|1=0x11, two DHT(20), SOS(12), EOI — identical bytes to fixtures.rs:104-119; the restart-interval splice at inspect.rs:59-77 matches fixtures.rs:127-145 exactly. Several local helpers (minimal_jpeg_with_sof_marker, zero_sof_jpeg) merely build on the local copy.
reduction: Delete the two local fns and use fixtures::minimal_baseline_jpeg / fixtures::minimal_baseline_jpeg_with_restart_interval (already in scope); repoint the derived helpers to the imported fixture. Remove the stale comment.
verify_note: inspect.rs:19-56 minimal_baseline_jpeg is byte-identical to fixtures.rs:103-119 (same DQT(67), SOF0 with 0x22/0x11 sampling written as (2<<4)|2 / (1<<4)|1, two DHT(20) with 0xAA/0xBB, SOS(12), EOI). inspect.rs:59-77 restart-interval splice matches fixtures.rs:127-145 exactly. j2k_test_support is already imported as `fixtures`, and its comment ('integration tests cannot access pub(crate) helpers') is stale since the fixture is public. minimal_jpeg_with_sof_marker and the derived helpers can repoint to the imported fixture. Two functions ~58 lines removable minus small delegating stubs; 55 slightly high.

title: APP14-RGB and progressive JPEG fixture byte-arrays duplicated in transcode tests
description: transcode/tests/fixtures/mod.rs inlines full JPEG byte-array fixtures rgb_app14_8x8_jpeg, rgb_app14_8x8_rgb and progressive_8x8_jpeg that already exist verbatim as pub fns in j2k-test-support (rgb_app14_8x8_jpeg, rgb_app14_8x8_rgb, progressive_8x8_jpeg). Ironically every other fixture in this same file is already a one-line delegate to j2k_test_support, so these three are inconsistent leftovers.
evidence: Compared byte arrays: mod.rs:51-76 rgb_app14 vector equals jpeg_fixtures.rs:3564-3589; mod.rs:81-86 rgb helper equals jpeg_fixtures.rs:3593-3598 (64x [200,20,10]); mod.rs:90-126 progressive vector equals jpeg_fixtures.rs:3603+. The crate already calls j2k_test_support at mod.rs:11-46.
reduction: Replace the three inline fixtures with delegating one-liners to j2k_test_support (matching the rest of the file), or drop the wrapper entirely and import directly.
verify_note: rgb_app14_8x8_jpeg byte vector at transcode fixtures/mod.rs:50-76 is byte-identical to jpeg_fixtures.rs:3562-3588; the rgb reference helper (64x [200,20,10]) at mod.rs:78-86 matches jpeg_fixtures.rs:3591-3598; progressive_8x8_jpeg at mod.rs:88-126 matches jpeg_fixtures.rs:3600+. The file already delegates every other fixture to j2k_test_support (mod.rs:11-46), so replacing these three with delegating one-liners is consistent and safe. Estimate is reasonable.

title: DWT parity max_abs_diff helper copied across 4 transcode GPU test files
description: A free-function max_abs_diff comparing the four DWT subbands (ll/hl/lh/hh) with dimension asserts is reimplemented in four test files. The Dwt53TwoDimensional variant (metal dct53.rs) is a straight duplicate of the existing pub method j2k_transcode::Dwt53TwoDimensional::max_abs_diff. The Dwt97TwoDimensional variant is reimplemented three times (metal dct97.rs, transcode dct97_2d.rs identical bodies; cuda dwt97_parity.rs equivalent via a band_max_diff helper) even though no equivalent method exists on Dwt97TwoDimensional in src.
evidence: src dct53_2d.rs:36-50 already provides pub fn max_abs_diff for Dwt53TwoDimensional; metal dct53.rs:339-354 duplicates it. metal dct97.rs:430-445 and transcode dct97_2d.rs:57-72 are character-identical; cuda dwt97_parity.rs:38-44 computes the same max via band_max_diff. Dwt97TwoDimensional has no such method (rg over src/ found only the Dwt53 and multilevel methods).
reduction: Add a pub `max_abs_diff` method to Dwt97TwoDimensional<f64> in j2k-transcode (mirroring the Dwt53 one), then delete all four local copies and call the methods (both GPU transcode crates depend on j2k-transcode).
verify_note: src dct53_2d.rs:35-50 already provides pub Dwt53TwoDimensional::max_abs_diff; metal dct53.rs:339-354 duplicates it verbatim. metal dct97.rs:430-445 and transcode dct97_2d.rs:57-72 are character-identical, and cuda dwt97_parity.rs:38-43 computes the same LL/HL/LH/HH max via band_max_diff. rg over crates/j2k-transcode/src confirms Dwt97TwoDimensional has NO max_abs_diff method (only Dwt53 2D and multilevel). Adding a pub max_abs_diff on Dwt97TwoDimensional<f64> and deleting the four copies is sound. But adding the ~18-line method offsets the ~58 lines removed, so net saving is closer to 40 than 55.

title: fnv1a64 digest helper copied into 5 test/bench/bin files
description: The FNV-1a 64-bit hex digest (offset basis 0xcbf29ce484222325, prime 0x100000001b3, format {:016x}) is re-typed in five places, all identical to the exported j2k_test_support::fnv1a64_hex. The two j2k-compare bins add an fnv1a64_hex_slices variant that is not in test-support.
evidence: test-support lib.rs:283-290 defines fnv1a64_hex; the cuda bench copies (htj2k_decode.rs:962-968, htj2k_encode.rs:982-988) and bench_harness.rs:859-865 are byte-identical; the compare bins wrap it as fnv1a64_hex(bytes)=fnv1a64_hex_slices(&[bytes]). j2k-compare (Cargo.toml:15) and j2k-cuda (Cargo.toml:57) both already depend on j2k-test-support.
reduction: Delete the local copies and call j2k_test_support::fnv1a64_hex; add a fnv1a64_hex_slices to test-support for the two compare bins that need the multi-slice form.
verify_note: test-support lib.rs:282-290 defines pub fnv1a64_hex. cuda benches htj2k_decode.rs:962-968 and htj2k_encode.rs:982-988 and compare tests/bench_harness.rs:859-865 are byte-identical copies. Both compare bins (jp2k_encode_compare.rs:2769-2782, jp2k_fixture_compare.rs:3807-3820) wrap it as fnv1a64_hex(bytes)=fnv1a64_hex_slices(&[bytes]) for a multi-slice form. j2k-compare (Cargo.toml:15) and j2k-cuda (Cargo.toml:57) both already depend on j2k-test-support. Deleting the identical copies and adding fnv1a64_hex_slices to test-support for the two bins is safe. Estimate reasonable.

title: PNM read/write/parse helpers duplicated between j2k-compare encode bin and test-support
description: jp2k_encode_compare.rs carries its own PnmImage struct, write_pnm, read_pnm, parse_pnm_u32 and a PnmParser (skip_ws_and_comments / token / skip_single_whitespace) that re-implement the P5/P6 writer, tokenizer and comment-skipping already provided by j2k-test-support (pnm_bytes/write_pnm/read_pnm_image plus its private read_pnm_token/skip_pnm_separators). Same-shape-different-detail: the bin uses String errors and adds a source_command field and an image-crate fallback path.
evidence: test-support lib.rs:184-343 implements write_pnm/read_pnm_image and an equivalent tokenizer; encode_compare.rs:2598-2620 write_pnm and 2661-2759 read_pnm+PnmParser mirror the same P5/P6 magic, maxval==255 check, single-whitespace skip and comment handling. j2k-compare already depends on j2k-test-support (Cargo.toml:15).
reduction: Have the bin reuse j2k_test_support::write_pnm and a shape-returning reader (read_pnm_image), keeping only the thin String-error/source_command adapter and the image-crate fallback. Delete the duplicated PnmParser/parse_pnm_u32.

title: j2k-transcode-metal pulls entire j2k-metal (43.5k LOC) for one type
description: j2k-transcode-metal declares a (macOS-target) dependency on the full j2k-metal crate but the only symbol it consumes is j2k_metal::MetalEncodedJ2k, referenced in a single function signature at lib.rs:157. Everything else Metal-related in the crate goes through the lightweight j2k-metal-support (metal.rs:12). This is backend-on-backend coupling: a transcode accelerator drags in the entire J2K decode/encode Metal backend (compute, buffer pools, ~30 modules, 8 shaders) to name one struct, inflating build graph and blurring the intended layering where transcode-metal should sit beside j2k-metal, not on top of it.
evidence: rg over crates/j2k-transcode-metal/src shows exactly one j2k_metal:: reference (lib.rs:157 `encoded: &j2k_metal::MetalEncodedJ2k`); all other Metal use is `use j2k_metal_support::{...}` (metal.rs:12). MetalEncodedJ2k is defined in crates/j2k-metal/src/encode/encoded.rs.
reduction: Move MetalEncodedJ2k (or a minimal encoded-buffer view type) into j2k-metal-support (or j2k-types), then drop the j2k-metal dependency from j2k-transcode-metal so it depends only on j2k-metal-support + j2k-transcode. Removes a 43.5k-LOC crate from this backend's build graph.
verify_note: Verified: `rg` over crates/j2k-transcode-metal/src shows exactly one j2k_metal:: reference (lib.rs:157, `encoded: &j2k_metal::MetalEncodedJ2k`); all other Metal plumbing goes through j2k_metal_support. The two methods actually called on the type — codestream_memory_range() and codestream_allocation_len() (encoded.rs:44,54) — only depend on metal::Buffer plus j2k_core::{BackendKind, DeviceMemoryRange}, and j2k-metal-support ALREADY depends on both `metal` and `j2k-core`, so a minimal view type there is feasible and would let the crate drop the heavy j2k-metal dep. The architectural defect is real and the fix is sound. Two caveats that don't change the verdict: (1) MetalEncodedJ2k is part of the stable public API (docs/stable-api-1.0.public-api.txt:3786), so the safe path is the finding's 'minimal view type' alternative, not physically moving the public type; (2) transcode-metal's own test (tests/route_report.rs) also constructs j2k_metal::MetalEncodedJ2k, so it too must migrate. Net SOURCE LOC deleted is near zero — the helper function stays and the type is added/moved rather than removed; the real payoff is removing a 43.5k-LOC crate from this backend's build graph, so est_loc_saved of 30 overstates code deletion.

title: Engine/facade split applied only to J2K, not JPEG or transcode
location: crates/j2k-native/ + crates/j2k/ vs crates/j2k-jpeg/ vs crates/j2k-transcode/
description: J2K uniquely separates a pure engine (j2k-native, 43,634 LOC, forbid unsafe, no j2k-core dep) from a facade (j2k, 9,971 LOC) that adapts it to j2k-core traits and hosts the pub `adapter` extension module. JPEG (j2k-jpeg, 30,772) and transcode (j2k-transcode, 10,207) each combine engine and facade in a single crate, yet j2k-jpeg has grown the same pub `adapter/` module shape (adapter/device_plan.rs, adapter/fast_packet.rs, adapter/baseline_encode.rs) that j2k has. Contributors must learn two different layouts for structurally parallel families. The split is well-justified for J2K (j2k-native is reused by 6 crates: j2k, j2k-cuda, j2k-metal, j2k-transcode, j2k-transcode-cuda, j2k-transcode-metal); j2k-jpeg's engine is reused by 4 crates (j2k-jpeg-cuda, j2k-jpeg-metal, j2k-transcode, j2k-transcode-metal), so the same reasoning nearly applies to it too.
evidence: j2k-jpeg/src has adapter/{mod,device_plan,fast_packet,baseline_encode}.rs mirroring j2k::adapter; but there is no j2k-jpeg-native engine crate — engine and facade coexist in j2k-jpeg. j2k-native explicitly does not depend on j2k-core (rg 'j2k_core' in j2k-native/src -> empty), unlike every other codec crate.
reduction: Pick one convention. Either (a) split j2k-jpeg into j2k-jpeg-native (engine) + j2k-jpeg (facade) to match J2K, so the 3x3 matrix is uniform; or (b) document that the engine/facade split is intentionally reserved for the largest reused engine and keep JPEG/transcode combined. Prefer (b) unless j2k-jpeg's build times or the 4 consumers force (a); the value is consistency, not LOC.

title: Two parallel contract crates: j2k-core (traits) and narrow j2k-types (encode-stage jobs)
description: The workspace has two contract crates. j2k-core is the broad trait/type hub every adapter implements. j2k-types is a separate no_std crate carrying only the encode-stage job/output structs (J2kForwardDwt*, J2kHt* jobs, Precomputed/Prequantized images, CpuOnlyJ2kEncodeStageAccelerator). It exists so j2k-native (which deliberately avoids j2k-core to stay a decoupled engine) and the j2k facade can share the encode-stage vocabulary without mirroring definitions. But it has only two consumers and its scope is essentially one adapter seam (encode_stage). This is a defensible boundary, but it means the 'contract layer' is bifurcated and a reviewer sees two crates doing conceptually one job.
evidence: `rg -l j2k_types` outside the crate returns exactly 2 files: j2k-native/src/lib.rs and j2k/src/adapter/encode_stage.rs. j2k-native has no j2k-core dependency (rg empty), forcing the separate types crate. j2k-types is 777 LOC of pure data structs (J2kForwardDwt53Job, PrecomputedHtj2k97Image, etc.).
reduction: Keep the split (it correctly preserves j2k-native's no_std/no-core-dep engine boundary) but consider relocating the accelerator job structs into a no_std submodule of j2k-core if j2k-native could tolerate depending on j2k-core; that would collapse two contract crates into one. If not, leave as-is and document that j2k-types is the engine<->facade encode seam so its narrowness reads as intentional.

title: Small shared crates are correctly factored — no over-granularity to fix there
description: The prompt flags j2k-metal-support (639) and by extension small crates as possibly too granular, but the evidence shows they are earning their keep and should NOT be merged. j2k-metal-support is consumed by all three Metal adapters (j2k-metal, j2k-jpeg-metal, j2k-transcode-metal) for device acquisition, route-profile labels, and MetalSupportError — genuine cross-adapter dedup. j2k-profile is consumed by native, jpeg, cuda, metal, jpeg-metal, and transcode-metal for shared timing/profiling. Merging either into a larger crate would create unnecessary coupling (e.g. forcing non-Metal crates to see Metal helpers, or forcing the metal-support helper to pull a heavy adapter). The only genuinely over-granular crate is the orphan j2k-tilecodec (separate finding).
evidence: `rg -l j2k_metal_support` -> j2k-metal, j2k-jpeg-metal, j2k-transcode-metal (3 distinct consumers). j2k-profile path-dep appears in native, jpeg, cuda, metal, jpeg-metal, transcode-metal Cargo.tomls. Both are #![doc(hidden)]/helper-scoped, so their public surface does not leak.
reduction: No action: retain j2k-metal-support and j2k-profile as-is. Direct granularity effort at the orphan j2k-tilecodec and at the missing j2k-metal-runtime consolidation instead.

title: ~2,900-line single function encode_ht_cleanup_code_blocks_with_runtime_and_statuses
description: A single free function spans from line 14200 to just before the next top-level fn at 17145 (~2,900 lines) with no intervening top-level items. It builds coefficient buffers, batch jobs, capacities, dispatches HT cleanup kernels, reads back statuses, and assembles results all inline — impossible to unit test or review in parts.
evidence: rg for '^fn ' finds no top-level fn between 14200 and 17145; awk fn-span measurement reports 2945 lines. Function opens at compute.rs:14200 'fn encode_ht_cleanup_code_blocks_with_runtime_and_statuses'; next top-level fn is dispatch_ht_cleanup at 17145.
reduction: Extract phases into named helpers: (1) job validation + coefficient/capacity packing, (2) buffer allocation, (3) command-buffer dispatch, (4) status readback/result assembly. Most already have sibling helpers in the compute/ subdir (encode_capacity.rs, direct_tier1.rs); move the packing/readback logic there and reduce this to an orchestrator of <200 lines.
verify_note: Verified: rg for top-level '^fn ' shows encode_ht_cleanup_code_blocks_with_runtime_and_statuses opens at compute.rs:14200 and the next top-level fn dispatch_ht_cleanup is at 17145 (2945 lines) with nothing in between. This is a genuine ~2.9k-line monolithic function. Sibling helper modules (encode_capacity.rs, direct_tier1.rs, etc.) already exist to receive extracted phases. The est_loc_saved of 300 is a modest, defensible NET reduction (most lines move rather than vanish, but boilerplate/validation dedup during extraction plausibly nets ~300).

title: j2k-metal/compute.rs is a 17.7k-LOC god-module with an incomplete extraction
description: The file declares 26 submodules (compute/*.rs) yet retains 17.7k LOC — the compute/ subdir totals only 7,397 LOC, so most logic (classic + HT encode pipelines, direct grayscale/color plans, stacked/repeated batch encoders, packing) still lives in the monolith. It is ~98% production code (only scattered #[cfg(test)] plus an external 'mod tests;' at 17750), so size is not test-driven.
evidence: wc: compute.rs=17757; compute/*.rs sum=7397 across 26 files (largest tests.rs 1435, abi.rs 905). Top production fns by span: encode_ht_cleanup...(2945), lossless_prepare_sizes(641), encode_stacked_direct_component_plane_batch(621), encode_prepared_direct_component_plane_in_command_buffer(559), encode_repeated_direct_grayscale_plan_in_command_buffer(407).
reduction: Continue the started decomposition: move the classic-encode family (prepare_classic_sub_band*, encode_classic_*), the HT-encode family (encode_ht_cleanup*, prepare_ht_sub_band*), and the direct/stacked/repeated color-plan encoders into dedicated compute/classic_encode.rs, compute/ht_encode.rs, compute/direct_encode.rs modules. Target <2k LOC per module.
verify_note: wc confirms compute.rs=17757 LOC; the compute/ subdir sums to 7397 LOC across 26 files, showing a decomposition already in progress but far from complete. The claimed large fns exist (encode_ht_cleanup 2945-line span verified). Continuing the split into classic/ht/direct encode modules is a real, sound architecture improvement. est 100 net is conservative and reasonable.

title: fast444 decode variants duplicate the shared subsampled generic path
description: For chroma-subsampled formats, fast420/fast422 variants are one-line forwarders into generic try_decode_fast_subsampled_*::<JpegFast4xxPacketV1> functions, but every fast444 variant carries its own full-length body (200-500 lines) rather than sharing the generic. This is a maintained copy of the batch/region/scaled/rgb/rgba orchestration for the 444 case.
evidence: compute.rs:6776 try_decode_fast422_region_scaled_rgb...with_output is a 3-line call to try_decode_fast_subsampled_region_scaled_rgb_batch_to_surfaces_with_output::<JpegFast422PacketV1>; compute.rs:4380 try_decode_fast444_full_rgb...with_output is a ~200-line body; other 444 bodies: region_scaled_rgb(279 @5076), region_scaled_rgba(250 @5550), full_rgba(201 @4677), scaled_region_to_surface(198 @8112). 43 total try_decode_fast* fns.
reduction: Make FastSubsampledMetal (or a sibling trait) cover the 444 packet type with a 1:1 chroma sampling factor, then delete the fast444 bodies and replace with generic forwarders like 420/422. Where 444's no-subsampling path truly differs, gate the small delta inside the generic rather than forking the whole function.
verify_note:
Verified the trait FastSubsampledMetal (compute.rs:819) is implemented only for JpegFast420PacketV1 (909) and JpegFast422PacketV1 (1141), NOT for 444. The 420/422 *_with_output fns are 3-line forwarders to the generic try_decode_fast_subsampled_*::

, whereas every fast444 batch body is a full fork (full_rgb ~297L @4380, full_rgba ~266L @4677, region_scaled_rgb ~398L @5076, region_scaled_rgba ~325L @5550, plus restart/grouped/to_surface variants). The generic body at 5998 is line-for-line parallel to the 444 body at 5076, and the trait already abstracts all pipeline selection plus chroma-boundary-repair as configurable hooks (TEXTURE_BOUNDARY_*, encode_extra_texture_repair_passes) that a 1:1-sampling 444 impl can supply/no-op. This is a genuine incomplete extraction, semantically mergeable without behavior/perf change (444 keeps its own kernels via the trait's pipeline getters). Total 444-specific batch body LOC comfortably exceeds 1500; est 900 net (after adding a ~150-line 444 trait impl) is realistic, possibly conservative.

title: Combinatorial thin-wrapper API in j2k-native encode.rs (precomputed/prequantized/preencoded)
description: The public encoder surface expands two independent axes (mct on/off, accelerator on/off) into 4 named functions per codestream flavor. encode_precomputed_{j2k,htj2k}_53 alone yields 8 public fns that each just call encode_precomputed_53_with_mct_and_accelerator with different bool/accelerator defaults. The htj2k_97 family adds prequantized/preencoded/owned/compact_owned permutations.
evidence: encode.rs:1300-1395 shows encode_precomputed_j2k_53, _with_accelerator, _with_mct, _with_mct_and_accelerator all delegating to encode_precomputed_53_with_mct_and_accelerator; same 4-way pattern repeats for htj2k_53 (1352-1395). rg lists 17 pub encode_precomputed/prequantized/preencoded entry points.
reduction: Collapse each family to one function taking an options/config struct (or builder) carrying use_mct + accelerator, keeping at most a CPU-default convenience wrapper. This removes ~12 delegation wrappers. Note: public API, so stage behind a deprecation window.

title: j2k-jpeg/decoder.rs god-module: 260 functions / 7.4k LOC with combinatorial decode wrappers
description: The decoder exposes a large matrix of entry points crossing axes {into, scaled, region, region_scaled, rgba8_with_alpha} x {, _with_scratch} x {, _with_options}, most of which are short forwarders, on top of several very large private cores. This makes the surface hard to navigate and the file hard to review.
evidence: rg counts 260 fns; 17 fns match decode*_with_scratch (scratch/non-scratch pairs). Largest fns: decode_region_into_output_format_with_scratch(256 @1442), decode_into_output_format_with_scratch(244 @928), output_format_from_parts(119 @5947), build_progressive_plan(151 @589). Public decode_into/decode/decode_scaled_into/... cluster at 839-1790.
reduction: Split into modules: plan-building (build_*_plan), the output-format cores (decode_*_output_format_with_scratch), and the public convenience surface. Collapse scratch/non-scratch and options/no-options pairs by giving cores an Option<&mut Scratch>/Options and letting a single generated set of wrappers default them.

title: Large embedded #[cfg(test)] modules bloat production files (jpeg-metal/lib.rs ~61%, cuda/encode.rs ~33%)
description: Several 'giant' files are inflated by inline test modules, distorting the perceived module size and slowing edits. lib.rs is ~4,500 test lines (61% of the file); cuda/encode.rs ~1,814 (33%); native encode.rs ~2,302 (21%).
